Name: Danielle Backman
Short_Bio: Danielle was born and raised in the Pacific Northwest, where camping trips and mountain hikes shaped her love for the wild. She has  climbed, skied, and dove across the world. She left her corporate job to live life more fully, and now works as a scuba dive guide and marine research assistant in the Caribbean.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I was born and raised in the Pacific Northwest and was privileged enough to have parents that took me outside from a young age. Vacations were spent camping along the Washington and Oregon coast, or hiking and camping in the cascades. What continued to draw me to the outdoors is the feeling of isolation and quietness. I love showing people my favorite places and helping others push themselves to do more than they thought they could. The feeling of accomplishment and exhaustion, as well ad the bliss I get from looking out into the wilderness and seeing only nature’s beauty is what keeps me coming back. The outdoors are my entire life, from backpacking to ski touring, trail running, scuba diving, mountaineering, and rock climbing - a day spent outdoors moving my body is what I live for. I quit my full time corporate job to travel and see more of the world’s beauty and have been doing thah the last 2 years. I have been scuba diving in raja ampat and the great barrier reef, rock climbing in Norway, ski touring in the French Alps, trekking in New Zealand, cross country skiing in Switzerland, and mountaineering all around the Washington and Canadian peaks. I now work as a scuba dive guide and research assistant at a marine research center in turks and caicos in the caribbean.
Professional_expertise_raw:
Scuba dive guide in Indonesia and Turks and Caicos
 Freelance underwater photographer and videographer 
 Backpacking trip leader for national nonprofit during college (SheJumps)
 Avid mountaineer 
 Backcountry skier - AIARE 1 - with 20 years experience skiing
 Naturalist at dive resort in Indonesia and Turks and Caicos
 Field research teaching assistant in turks and caicos
Travel_types_experience:
Have been solo traveling the last 2 years around Europe, Australia, New Zealand, Southeast Asia and Canada. 
 

 Led beginner backpacking groups in Colorado. 
 

 

 Have encouraged and given tips for friends on solo traveling as well as given resources for and taught backcountry skiing, backpacking, mountaineering, rock climbing and scuba diving.
 

 Assisted teaching first time scuba divers

 Indonesia: Sulwesi: South Sulawesi: Bira

Fav_outdoor_experiences: My favorite trip so far has been my successful summit attempt of Mt Rainier in Washington. It took 3 attempts over 3 years as a result of people in my group having altitude sickness. Growing up looking at the mountain i always wanted to climb it. I was leading my rope team and it was a long, cold, windy day and I was feeling the altitude and exhaustion but i pushed myself through and finally reached the summit with my team. This mountain will always have a special place in my heart as it taught me patience, determination, resilience, the importance of friendship and showed me how small we really are.

Name: Sunny Stroeer
Short_Bio:
Sunny is a record-setting adventure athlete, backcountry guide, and expedition leader who thrives in the wildest, most remote places on Earth. From speed records in the Grand Canyon to leading all-women summits in the Andes, her life’s work is helping people push their limits—and discover what they’re truly capable of in the outdoors.

She is also the first woman to ski the full 1,000-mile Iditarod Trail. And when she's not in Alaska, you’ll find her guiding mountaineering expeditions in the Andes, running Dreamland Safari Tours in Utah, or leading high-altitude adventures through AWE Expeditions, my women-focused expedition company.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: What drew me to the outdoors? The transformative nature of unplugging and going after big adventures in awe-inspiring surroundings. Today, I'm a record setting adventure athlete, backcountry & mountain guide, and expedition company owner.
Professional_expertise_raw: I specialize in difficult backpacking (think remote, rugged, off trail), high altitude mountaineering, and very long distances. I am the first woman to have skied the 1000 mile Iditarod Trail (though I'm not much of a cross country skier!), have set speed records around the Annapurna Circuit, on 22,838ft Aconcagua, and in the Grand Canyon, and I LOVE leading all-women trips for www.awexpeditions.org (and co-ed ones for www.dreamlandtours.net too).
Travel_types_experience: See above.
Geographic_areas_raw: Utah, California, Andes, Mendoza, Khumbu, Everest Region, Annapurna Region, Cordillera Blanca, Sacred Valley, Alaska
Fav_outdoor_experiences: Too many to answer! The Iditarod Trail was a pretty good one. https://www.abc4.com/news/local-news/first-woman-to-ski-1000-mile-trail-recounts-death-defying-trek-across-alaskan-backcountry/

Name: Codell Norman
Short_Bio: Codell grew up in the Southwest and, after serving in the military, knew he needed a life built around the outdoors. He started guiding Grand Canyon trips over a decade ago—and never left. Today, he's the Senior Operations Coordinator for the largest commercial hiking program in the U.S., but he still lights up every time I get to talk trip planning, logistics, and routes with someone who’s excited about the desert.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: Growing up in the Southwest US, I was expected to be outside. After the military I learned I needed to continue in a career based in outdoor recreation. Now I help manage a commercial. Now I am the Senior Operations Coordinator for the largest program of the largest commercial hiking operation in the country.
Professional_expertise_raw:
Guided 100+ backcountry multiday trips in the Northern Arizona and Southern Utah region.
 

 Scouted and considered other locations such as Peru and Pacific Northwest US but couldn't leave The Grand Canyon and realized I'm just a desert lizard at heart.
Travel_types_experience: In my career development and after the pandemic, I SOLD quite a few and operated private Grand Canyon for a local company here in Flagstaff that helped me get my foot in the door. Ranging in all facets Grand Canyon tourism, above and below the rim.
Geographic_areas_raw:
American South West - Grand Canyon Region including deep experience with how to get permits for Havasupai and the National Park. experience with both of those locations as Back Country Guide for 10± year including Phone Sales discussion. Sedona, AZ, Paria Canyon, Death Valley, Zion, and Canyonlands. Also many other minor public lands of the region.
 

 Lima, Peru
 Cusco, Peru
Fav_outdoor_experiences:
I hiked the Butte Fault to High Saddles route with 2 guides I was hired with and grew close with as our careers matured over 10 years. The route is highly coveted by the upper ranger of canyon hikers and an incredible feat of general ability all together. 
 https://caltopo.com/m/1C9K9
 

 This was after leaving the industry for a year during the pandemic for construction when seeing the writing on the wall, we as operators were not going to survive the economy, then returning in a last ditch effort to save my outdoor career.
Other_hobbies_interests:
DnD and other Tabletop Games
 Bass Guitar 
 Sewing
 Electronic tinkering. (High level of understanding of electrical theory and practice from father, military service, and construction jobs. Now I use it to build and modify my bass rig and have built a low effort, highly functional digital gaming board.
Languages_raw: Enough Spanish to get me in trouble, get fed, get a bathroom, and get lost.

Name: Stephanie Hatley
Short_Bio: Stephanie is a full-time traveler, outdoor lover, and mom of two. Since 2021, her family has been on the road in their RV, exploring national parks, working seasonal jobs, and soaking in the beauty of the U.S. together. She's hiked hundreds of trails with elementary-age kids, and knows the highs (and challenges!) of planning great trips as a family.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: Our family has traveled the US in our RV since 2021 - visiting national parks, learning about our history, and taking in breathtaking scenery. We understand the traveling family, whether it’s full-timers, weekend warriors, or summer vacationers. We absolutely love travel and sharing our experiences with others. Plus, being able to do this with our kids makes our lifestyle even more memorable!
Professional_expertise_raw: I wouldn’t consider myself a professional at anything. But with our experience, I know that I have valuable insight to offer families. My background is in education and ministry, so connecting with people, listening, and helping guide people come naturally.
Travel_types_experience: I have worked at a destination campground outside Acadia National Park and spent months giving suggestions and advice to tourists. Most of these visitors were there for the first time and on a short visit, so maximizing their time was important to me. Obviously, having traveled the country and hiked numerous trails with elementary-age kids, I have a good idea about family travel as well.
Geographic_areas_raw:
United States: National Parks: Acadia
 United States: North Carolina: Western and Piedmont NC
 United States: Utah: Might Five
Fav_outdoor_experiences:
Our family took a trip to the Bay of Fundy last summer and splurged on a sea kayaking adventure along the magnificent rock formations. We had a picnic on a beach afterwards, and it was breathtaking to witness the largest tides in the world!
 

 Our family has worked and lived just outside Acadia National Park for the last two summers. The first time my husband and I hiked the Beehive Trail, we loved it! We even took friends twice and recommended it to all visitors without a fear of heights who were physically capable. The views of the Atlantic and Sand Beach are just stunning!
 

 Our adventure through the Fiery Furnace in Arches National Park is one I’ll never forget, and it’s one of my kids’ favorite memories of our travels so far. It’s literally an outdoor playground where you don’t have to follow a specific path and can enjoy jumping of rocks and shimmying through slot canyons like you’re a kid again!
Other_hobbies_interests: Music, writing, teaching, reading
Traveler_types: Families with young kids,First-time adventurers

Name: Lura Snow
Short_Bio: Lura has spent her entire life exploring the outdoors, from childhood camping trips to 16 years as a professional guide across Utah and Arizona. Now, as the Utah Operations Manager for a local trekking company, she oversee multi-day backpacking and basecamp trips through the region’s most stunning landscapes, including Zion, Bryce Canyon, Arches, and the Grand Canyon.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I grew up in a very outdoorsy family, I have been hiking and camping since I was a baby. I was a river/climbing/canyoneering/naturalist/jeep guide for 16 years. I am currently an operations manager for large guide company in Southern Utah. I still spend all of my time off camping, hiking, backpacking, and paddling.
Professional_expertise_raw: I am the Utah Operations Manager for the Wildland Trekking Company, we run multi day backpacking and basecamp trips in the Mighty 5 National Parks in Utah, Grand Canyon, Death Valley, Joshua Tree, Grand Staircase, Vermillion Cliffs National Monument, and other public lands throughout Utah. I was a guide on these lands for 16 years before stepping into an administrative role.
Travel_types_experience: I have experience with all of these groups in my work as a guide and in my management role.
Geographic_areas_raw: Utah, Arizona
Fav_outdoor_experiences:
Every time I have a friend come to visit we hike the Narrows in Zion. I know it has a reputation of being extremely crowded, but there are several ways to avoid the crowds, and I know the best times of year to head up there. I love showing folks the majesty of the Narrows. 
 

 My favorite personal trip was driving and hiking the White Rim in Moab over three days and getting to explore some of the very remote back country areas of Canyonlands National Park.

Name: Robin S. deWolfe
Short_Bio: Since retiring in 2019, Robin  volunteered in national parks across the U.S., helping visitors plan their trips and discover hidden gems along the way.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors:
I spent 35 years working in the mental health field working as a therapist. Besides my masters in counseling, I also have one in Zoology with an emphasis on animal behavior and ornithology(I am an avid bird watcher). Throughout my career, I spent many of my vacations traveling worldwide to see animals in their natural habitat i.e. Kenya, Antarctica, Uganda, Madagascar, Galapagos to name a few or going on adventures in our National Parks.  
 When I retired in 2019, I had already decided I would become a volunteer for NPS so I could live in and help visitors find the same excitement for the parks that I've had. I have now volunteered in Yellowstone, Sequoia, Hot Springs, Wupatki, Walnut Canyon, Rocky Mountain,the Everglades, Katmai, Arches, Bryce, Mesa Verde, and at the Mt Baker/Snoqualamie National Forest. I work in their visitor centers helping people with information so they can make the most of their visit. I also saw my role as helping them discover other hidden treasures they might find on their way to their next destination.
 I'm an avid road biker, kayaker, and hiker.  
 At one time, I lived in Blanding, UT and I've lived in Flagstaff, AZ for the last 19 years, The four corners area and the whole of the SW is my forte.
 Helping people figure out their travel plans is right up my alley!
 I was supposed to go to Acadia this summer and live in their Lighthouse but due to everything going on in the Park service, they decided not to open up the Lighthouse this summer to visitors.
 So my plan is to stay at home and volunteer at Walnut Canyon and our new Astronomy Discovery Center, which just opened in November of last year.
Professional_expertise_raw: I have been a volunteer Park Ranger for the last 6 years. I have helped people make plans as they've traveled to another park. I am certified in Wilderness First Aid.
Travel_types_experience: These are the kinds of visitors, I have worked with in the parks. Many visitors asked if I'd accompany them on their journey.
Geographic_areas_raw:
United States: Arizona: Flagstaff area: all Three Monuments: Petrified Forest: Canyon de Chelly: Navajo National Monument:Page: Rainbow Bridge: Grand Canyon: Kaibab Trail: North Rim: Route 89 to Kanab with a stop at the Toadstools on the way and possible a stop to see if the person can get a reservation to go to the Wave!
 United States: Utah: Zion:Cedar Breaks: Bryce: Grand Staircase Escalante: Capitol Reef: Bentonite Hills: Goblin State Park: Canyonlands, Arches: Natural Bridges: Bears Ears (house on Fire): Hovenweep: Monument Valley
 United States: Colorado: Cortez: Mesa Verde: Durango: Canyon of the Ancients: Black Canyon of the Gunnison: Rocky Mountain National Park
 United States: Florida: Everglades: Big Cypress Preserve Loop road: Shark Valley: Mangrove Swamps
Fav_outdoor_experiences: Hiking through Isle Royal, Kayaking in Antarctica with the penguins, Bird watching and kayaking in the Everglades, hiking the Inca trail, in Peru, Kayaking through the bioluminescence off of Bellingham, WA, Bird watching in Ecuador during the Audubon Christmas bird count, watching the bears fish for salmon, in Katmai
Other_hobbies_interests:
I make it my business to know where to find the best homemade ice cream where ever I go.
 Reading

 Bird watching

Name: Misty Lopez
Profession: Active travel guide and outdoor educator
Short_Bio: Misty is a travel planner based in Lake Atitlan, Guatemala. She's spent the last six years planning and leading community hikes, volcano treks, and women’s outdoor experiences around Lake Atitlán, helping others feel connected, confident, and curious along the way.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors:
I didn’t grow up hiking or traveling, but something in me always craved movement, curiosity, and connection to nature. It wasn’t until my early 20s, and especially after moving to Guatemala, that I really fell in love with the outdoors. Here, surrounded by volcanoes, lakes, and trails woven into daily life, I began to experience nature not as a backdrop, but as a teacher and a reset button.
 

 The outdoors has become where I return to myself. It’s where I feel most grounded, most alive, and most in awe. It challenges me physically and emotionally, but it also reminds me of my strength and resilience. Whether I’m hiking up a volcano, paddleboarding on Lake Atitlán, or running through the mountains, the outdoors reconnects me to something deeper. I'd love to be able to share that!
Professional_expertise_raw:
I’, from the U.S., but moved to Guatemala for six years ago. I've spent this time deeply connected to both the land and local communities. I’ve coordinated tours with women artisan groups, supported local adventure agencies with marketing and outreach, and organized volcano hikes for U.S.-based nonprofits visiting the region.
 

 I also run a women’s adventure group where I plan and lead local experiences around Lake Atitlán for women living at the lake (expats and locals alike), from community hikes to day-long outdoor adventures. My focus is always on creating meaningful and uplifting outdoor experiences that connect people to nature, themselves, and each other.
Travel_types_experience:
I’ve helped over 15 groups — including solo travelers, couples, and small families with young kids — plan their travel to Guatemala. I support them with everything from packing lists and weather prep to choosing the right lodging, activities, and local agencies or guides.
 

 For first-time adventurers and solo travelers especially, I focus on making them feel confident and supported. I’ve even accompanied some solo visitors on hikes or local outings when they felt too alone or unsure, helping them feel more connected and safe.
 

 My goal is always to provide thoughtful, personalized guidance so travelers can experience the beauty of Guatemala with peace of mind and a sense of adventure.
Geographic_areas_raw: Guatemala: Sololá Department: Lake Atitlán: Panajachel: Santa Cruz La Laguna: San Juan La Laguna: Santiago Atitlán
Fav_outdoor_experiences: Some of my favorite outdoor experiences in Guatemala include hiking to the hidden waterfalls in Santiago and camping nearby — it's peaceful, lush, and full of magic. I also loved the challenge of hiking both Atitlán and Tolimán volcanoes back-to-back. It was truly rewarding! And one of my most beautiful adventures was circumnavigating Lake Atitlán by kayak over four days — an unforgettable way to connect with yourself and the rhythms of the lake.
Other_hobbies_interests: Outside of being outside, I love storytelling, whether through photography or writing. I'm passionate about community and finding ways to support local artisans and small businesses. I also enjoy cooking (especially anything with Mediterranean flavors), and spending time in cozy cafés!
Languages_raw: Aside from native English, I also speak very fluent Spanish. I lost a little vocabulary growing up in the States, but living in Guatemala has improved it :)
Traveler_types: Solo travelers,Families with young kids,First-time adventurers
Describe_you_select_all: Outdoor Educator/Instructor,Travel Advisor or Trip Designer

Name: Mary Andino
Short_Bio:
Mary is a backpacker, climber, writer, and gear nerd. She started hiking as a teenager and has spent the years since chasing long-distance trails, rock faces, and meaningful stories from the backcountry. When she's not climbing or out on trail, she write for publications like Backpacker, Climbing, and Outside, covering everything from new ultralight gear to first-person trail narratives.

She  lived in Italy while pursuing a PhD in Italian history and hiked 350 miles from Florence to Rome along the Cammino di Francesco.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors:
I grew up in an outdoorsy family, and started backpacking when I was a teenager. As an adult, backpacking and climbing are my two greatest passions. When I'm not working, I'm outside on my latest sport project or on a backcountry trail. I've done the Camino de Santiago in Spain and Cammino di Francesco in Italy (350 miles each), and trails in Bryce Canyon, California, Michigan, and Joshua Tree. I climb outdoors across the country and also work as a coach at a climbing gym. 
 The outdoors is also key to my professional life. I regularly write about backpacking, climbing, and gear for publications such as Backpacker, Climbing, Outside, GearJunkie, and Terrain. I write about everything from National Parks news to first-hand accounts of my trips with advice to innovations in ultralight gear. I am well-versed about communicating about the outdoors to a variety of audiences. 
 Links to my work below: 
 https://www.backpacker.com/byline/mary-andino/
 https://www.climbing.com/byline/mary-andino/
 https://gearjunkie.com/author/maryandino
Professional_expertise_raw:
As an author for multiple outdoor brands, I write both about my personal travel experiences and news in the outdoor world. I write for everyone from the seasoned thru-hiker to the novice looking to try their first overnight hike in the backcountry. I bring a strong element of gear expertise as well. I meticulously test and write about camping, hiking, and climbing gear and know which products are best for the right user. 
 I have a broad expertise in Italy. I lived in Italy for a year while pursuing a PhD in Italian history and am fluent in the language. I also hiked 350 miles through the country, from Florence to Rome. I feel particularly confident advising travelers about this country.
Travel_types_experience: As a solo female backpacker, I understand how to prioritize safety in the backcountry and alleviate concerns people might have. I regularly take newbies backpacking and climbing and am patient and empathetic helping people navigate nature for the first time.
Geographic_areas_raw:
Midwest: Climbing: Jackson Falls, Sam's Throne, The Red, Chattanooga, Black Hills. 
 Italy: Umbria and Tuscany, The South: Naples, Sicily, Bari, 
 National Parks: Bryce Canyon National Park, Zion National Park, Joshua Tree National Park, Badlands National Park 
 California: Sierra Nevadas, Dinkey Lakes Wilderness
Fav_outdoor_experiences:
I really enjoyed the California Riding and Hiking Trail in Joshua Tree National Park. I was there over Thanksgiving weekend, and the park was slammed with people, but I only saw 4 people on this 37-mile backcountry trail. It offered the best of the park without the crowds. 
 

 The Camino de Santiago was a magical experience. I took the Norte route, which went from Santander to Santiago de Compostela. I was able to experience small, remote towns in Spain that a tourist would never get to see. I met people from all over the world who were doing the hike and built a community on trail.
Other_hobbies_interests: I love yoga and reading. I also work as a food writer and editor, so I'm always in the kitchen cooking up something.
Traveler_types: Solo travelers,First-time adventurers

Name: Kate Sedrowski
Short_Bio: Kate is a climber, outdoor writer, and adventure coach with experience across Colorado, Utah, Wyoming, California, and Arizona. She create trail guides, gear reviews, and climbing resources, and is an AMGA-certified Single Pitch Instructor. She also work with first-time adventurers and women over 60 to help them feel confident outdoors.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: Nearly 10 years ago, I fell head over heel hooks for climbing and moved to Colorado – never having set foot in the state – because the mountains were calling. Now my life is centered around outdoor adventures, both in my free time and as a freelance writer in the outdoor space.
Professional_expertise_raw: I primarily work as a writer, creating location guides, trail guides, gear guides, and other blog posts about climbing and hiking. I also recently became an AMGA-certified Single Pitch Instructor to share my love of climbing.
Travel_types_experience: I myself am an avid solo traveler, and I love to share my knowledge with others who are going it alone. I also got into climbing somewhat later in life, so I am passionate about helping people try activities they are a little intimidated by to discover what they are capable of. I work as an adventure coach for a small company that helps empower women to get outside, so I have firsthand experience supporting first time adventurers and women who are over age 60.
Geographic_areas_raw: Colorado, Utah, Wyoming, California
Fav_outdoor_experiences: It's too hard to pick just one! Some of my most memorable adventures include hiking the Grand Canyon Rim-to-Rim in a day, climbing in Zion National Park, summitting Devils Tower in Wyoming (twice!), and camping, hiking, and climbing many times in Joshua Tree National Park.
Languages_raw: No, unfortunately. I know very basic Spanish and even less French.

Name: Emily Schrick
Short_Bio: Emily has thru-hiked the Pacific Crest Trail, trekked Patagonia’s O Circuit, and explored hundreds of miles of backcountry in the Sierra. For 6 years, she worked at REI helping people prep for adventures big and small—whether they were gearing up for the John Muir Trail or just starting to car camp. She is currently based in San Francisco, and can help with any urban adventure as well
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I found the outdoors in college by day hiking and photography and it led to so much more! Thru-hiking the PCT, traveling to Patagonia for the O Circuit - my life would not be the same without the outdoors
Professional_expertise_raw: I worked at REI for 6 years helping customers on the sales floor prep for their trips and also teaching in-store classes about specific destinations and hiking, backpacking, and camping skills.
Travel_types_experience:
As a solo traveler myself, I have lots of guidance to give to women especially about traveling, hiking, and backpacking by yourself. I frequently also travel with my parents aged 60+ to National Parks and have a general idea of what people in that demographic are looking for.
 I'm not entirely sure what constitutes "extreme athletes" but I'd be happy to talk more about thru-hiking and long distance backpacking as I have experience on multiple long trails - PCT, CT, JMT, etc.

United States: California: Yosemite National Park
 United States: California: Yosemite National Park: Half Dome
 United States: California: Yosemite National Park: Cloud's Rest
 United States: California: Sierra Nevada

Name: Julie Cleveland
Profession: Park ranger and active travel guide
Short_Bio: Julie has built her life around the outdoors—working in national parks, exploring backcountry trails, and finding connection in wild places around the world. She currently works full-time for the Bridger-Teton National Forest, and over the last decade has held seasonal roles with the Forest Service and NPS in places like Glacier, Yellowstone, and the North Cascades.Outside of work, she's traveled solo across 19 countries, taught yoga in India, and backpacked my her through some of the most remote corners of the U.S. and New Zealand.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors:
After graduating from college in 2013, I spent two summers working in a remote mountain town, Stehekin, in the North Cascades, Washington. I completely fell in love with the outdoors during this time and spent my days off hiking, backpacking, fishing, swimming, biking, and exploring. After doing two years of AmeriCorps (2014-2016), then seven months of traveling abroad solo (2016-2017), I started working seasonally for the Forest Service and National Park Service in summers, and ski resorts in winters. I have worked in the Tongass National Forest (Alaska), Glacier National Park, North Cascades National Park, Yellowstone National Park, Big Sky Ski Resort, Grand Targhee Ski Resort, Stevens Pass Ski Resort, and Abel Tasman National Park in New Zealand. Now, I have a permanent full-time job with the Bridger-Teton National Forest in Wyoming. 
 Through all this time and to this day, I spend as much time as I can outdoors. My favorite activities are hiking, camping, backpacking, floating (rafting, paddle boarding, canoeing, and kayaking), fishing, biking, snowboarding, xc skiing, and traveling (domestically and internationally). I have traveled to 19 countries, and over half of the United States. 
 Spending time outdoors and connecting with nature is extremely valuable to mental and emotional health and wellness, and I would be lost without it.
Professional_expertise_raw:
For the last eight years, I worked for public lands (Forest Service and National Park Service) and have thoroughly explored all the places I worked. As mentioned in my previous response, I have experience in Alaska, Washington, Montana, Wyoming, Idaho, New Zealand, and various other states and countries. 
 I also earned my yoga instructor certification in India in 2017 and I still regularly teach classes.
Travel_types_experience:
When issuing backcountry permits in Glacier National Park in 2018 and 2019, I helped people of all ages and experience levels plan backpacking trips. When working as a snowboard instructor at Big Sky Resort and Grand Targhee Resort, I taught people of all ages and ability levels how to snowboard. 
 I have been a solo traveler since 2016, and in my travels I have connected with all kinds of people, especially other solo travelers. Working in public lands, many of the visitors I have come across are from all demographics looking for various activities of varying experience levels. National parks attract a lot of families with young kids, multi-generational groups, travelers aged 60+, and first-time adventurers. 
 Anytime someone I know visits an area I'm familiar with, I am always excited to give them recommendations and help them plan their trips to give them the best experience possible, and I always tailor this advice to their experience level and interests.

Fav_outdoor_experiences:
I spent 8 days on a backcountry horse-packing trip in the Thorofare in Yellowstone, the most remote place in the lower 48. 
 I worked as a backcountry hut ranger in Abel Tasman National Park, working for 8 days at a time in the backcountry at the Whariwharangi Hut, then having 6 days off to explore the rest of the country.
 I spent nearly all my 3-day weekends in Glacier National Park backpacking solo. 
 I traveled solo for 7 months around Thailand, Bali, Australia, New Zealand, India, and Ghana.
 I completed my yoga teacher training in India.
 I spent two summers working in Stehekin, WA, the most remote town in the state, only accessible by boat, plane, or hiking (no roads lead to Stehekin).

Name: Zoe Woods
Short_Bio: Zoe spent years working as a National Park Ranger, hiking guide, and outdoor educator, helping people experience some of the most incredible landscapes in North America. She knows Rocky Mountain, Glacier, and Alaska’s vast wilderness inside and out
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I grew up in Colorado so an early love for the mountains was instilled in me. I hiked and camped with my family, but it wasn't until college that I discovered backpacking. Right out of college, I got a job working in Rocky Mountain National Park, which has propelled me into the field of environmental education and outdoor recreation where I currently work. Today, the outdoors is my sanctuary. As a queer person, I feel safe in the outdoors in a way I never felt around my family or friends. I've experienced first-hand the ways that nature can be healing and restorative. As our world continues to change around us and climate change starts to take a toll on our mental health, I see nature as the way to reconnect and disconnect. I want to help others find this same solace that I found.
Professional_expertise_raw: I worked at Rocky Mountain National Park as an interpretive ranger intern, and hiked nearly all the trails in the park while I was there. At Glacier National Park, I was a hiking and backpacking guide. I chased mountains as far west as I could and now I live in Alaska where I worked as a forest ranger for the Chugach National Forest. In the winters, I work as a ski instructor and have worked for 2 seasons at Copper Mountain in Colorado and 1 season at Alyeska Resort in Alaska. I am a wilderness first responder and a certified master naturalist.
Travel_types_experience: Working at the Beaver Meadows Visitor Center in Rocky Mountain National Park, I understand intimately the trails that the park has to offer and can personally attest to their difficulty and specify what would fit the abilities of kids, all the way up to seniors. In Glacier, I regularly took families and solo hikers on guided hikes, and led driving tours for groups of mixed abilities. In a similar vein to RMNP, I worked at the Begich Boggs Visitor Center in Alaska, which was essentially the frontlines for trip planning in Alaska as it is one of the only easily accessible information stations. There, I provided recommendations to all sorts of visitors, domestic and foreign, experienced and novice, families and singles.
Geographic_areas_raw:
United States: Colorado: Rocky Mountain National Park
 United States: Montana: Glacier National Park
 United States: Alaska: Southeastern Alaska
 United States/Canada: Alaska-Canada Highway Route
Fav_outdoor_experiences: Paddleboarding out to a glacier. The grueling bike trip up the Going to the Sun Road in Glacier NP. A humpback whale nearly flipping our sea kayak when we were in Glacier Bay NP: it was so close we could feel the spray from its blowhole. Watching the northern lights deep in the Yukon on my roadtrip from Denver to Alaska.
Other_hobbies_interests: I am a certified sommelier! I love wine and the stories it tells about history and the land. I'm a huge foodie (I actually used to do food and wine tours in Denver!). Right now, I'm trying desperately to find someone who has some moose I can try. I do some freelance writing; I'm working on a piece for Thrillist on the glacier tourism industry right now. I'm an (amateur) poet, with a couple publishing credits under my belt.
Languages_raw: I can speak Spanish to a professional fluency. I lived in Madrid, and regularly worked with Spanish visitors and teach ski lessons in Spanish.

Name: Bridget Murray
Short_Bio: Bridget spent many seasons with the National Park Service as a science and resource ranger, and now works as an outdoor recreation planner for the Bureau of Land Management in Utah. Her work has ranged from writing interpretive content and permit systems to mapping obscure campsites, designing signage, and partnering with local guides.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I stumbled my way into outdoor recreation via outdoor work when I was 23 and stumbled into a job in Moab, UT. In the year, since I have dabed in countless activities, lived all over the country, and had the immense privilege of developing my skills and knowledge of the mountain west through work. I currently work in outdoor recreation planning for the government, and spend most of my weekends adventuring outdoors. My forays into hiking, then backpacking, then rock climbing, caving, rafting, and more have brought me nose to nose with the greatest challenges and rewards life has to offer: challenges, growth, learning, friendships, stunning views and a spiritual connection to the world around me.
Professional_expertise_raw: I worked for three seasons for the National Park Service as a science and resource management ranger- think, super cool backcountry fieldwork and lots of learning about everything from lichens to Mexican Spotted Owls. I now work for the Bureau of Land Management in Utah as an outdoor recreation planner. My job varies throughout the year, week, and even day and has included writing educational content, designing signs, writing compliance documents, administering commercial use permits, developing park ranger projects, and meeting some of the most fascinating recreationists while out and about.
Travel_types_experience: Coming from the east coast, I had no network of mentors or friends to learn from when I ventured into the world of outdoor recreation. For years, I was a self-taught solo traveler, and can understand the unique nuances and challenges that clients might be facing. Being that my family does not spend much time outdoors, I am also well acquainted with planning trips for parties with diverse needs including physical disabilities or limitations, children, and of course the obligatory head-butting when six people want six different things— can you tell I’m my family’s resident travel advisor? Finally, in the past year I have taken more forays into organizing group trips (backpacking boating and canyoneering) both on my own, and with an area outdoors club. These trips are more specialized with some non-negotiable requirements (as I am a trip leader, not a paid and insured guide), but I also specifically choose locations and itineraries that are accommodating of first time adventures and work hard to make sure they feel welcomed and included.
Geographic_areas_raw:
Utah: essentially the entirety of Emery County- the San Rafael Swell and surrounding area, down to the most obscure campsites and historic mines. Backpacking, off-roading, climbing, you name it. Grand Staircase: Hole in the rock road trails and Escalante River trail. Moab: Arches, Needles, Canyonlands, and other area parks/monuments, hiking and sightseeing. I’m also familiar with many guides in Moab as I administer their commercial use permits. Zion: Subway, Kolob Canyon trails, West Rim Trail. St George: local climbing and hiking opportunities. 
 Colorado: White River NF: Western slope district (Flat Tops). Grand Junction: Colorado National Monument, Dominguez Escalante NCA: Dominguez Canyon Wilderness and the Gunnison river.
 Arizona: Flagstaff: area trails. Grand Canyon: oodles of backpacking, both rims and the river. Sedona; area trails. Phoenix: Saguaro day hikes and backpacking.
 California/Oregon: southern cascades/northern Sierras east to the Warner Mountains. 
 Nevada: Great Basin NP: alpine lakes trail, and wheeler peak glacier
 Georgia: Cumberland Island National Seashore: every trail (no exaggeration), Okefenokee Swamp paddling

Most beautiful: Escalante route in the Grand Canyon; floating the Grand Canyon; hiking in the Grand Canyon of the Tuolomne River.

Name: Dehn Craig
Short_Bio: Dehn is trail runner, filmmaker, father of five, and lifelong adventurer. He grew up in Lake Tahoe, where backcountry routes and alpine lakes were part of daily life. That love for wild places inspired him to launch Vacation Races, a running event series near national parks—and later, Global Adventures, where I designed multi-day travel experiences all over the world.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors:
The outdoors has been woven into the tapestry of my life from the beginning. As a kid, backpacking trips with my family taught me resilience, wonder, and the thrill of discovery. Growing up in Lake Tahoe, adventure wasn’t an event—it was the backdrop of everyday life. That love for wild places led me to start Vacation Races (a company that organized running events near National Parks), where my greatest joy was helping runners—many visiting for the first time—craft their own unforgettable experiences in nature.
 

 That passion for exploration deepened when I spent two years living in a motorhome with my wife and five kids, crisscrossing the U.S., checking off (and adding to) a never-ending bucket list. Now, I take my own family backpacking and lead youth groups into the wilderness, guiding them as they push past their limits and experience the transformative power of wild places.
 

 Mark Twain said, “Travel is fatal to prejudice, bigotry, and narrow-mindedness.” I’ve seen firsthand how stepping into the unknown—whether a remote canyon or a vast alpine meadow—expands our perspectives. Edward Abbey was right: “Wilderness is not a luxury but a necessity.” It’s where we reconnect, reset, and remember what truly matters.
 

 For me, adventure isn’t just a pastime—it’s a calling. And helping others find their own path into the wild? That’s the best part.
Professional_expertise_raw:
My professional expertise is rooted in creating experiences that connect people to places in meaningful ways. As the founder of Vacation Races, I built a running event series that not only brought participants to breathtaking locations near national parks but also helped them craft personalized itineraries—curating hikes, viewpoints, restaurants, and activities tailored to their interests and abilities. It was never just about the race; it was about the full experience.
 

 Expanding on that idea, I launched Vacation Races Global Adventures, designing 7–9 day international trips that combined running with immersive cultural experiences. I collaborated with local guides, park rangers, and hospitality experts to create authentic itineraries—blending adventure, history, and local flavors in a way that resonated deeply with our participants.
 

 Earlier in my life, I spent two years as a volunteer missionary in Japan, learning the language, adapting to new environments, and developing a deep appreciation for different cultures. That experience shaped my ability to connect with people, solve problems on the fly, and communicate across cultural boundaries.
 

 At my core, I’m driven by customer experience design—listening, understanding, and crafting journeys that leave a lasting impact. Whether through itinerary building, logistics coordination, or direct customer support, I thrive on creating moments that help people experience the world in a way that’s personal, seamless, and unforgettable.
Travel_types_experience:
I have extensive experience advising a wide range of travelers, from solo explorers to families with young kids, multi-generational groups, and even extreme athletes. My confidence in guiding these groups comes from both professional experience and personal adventures across the U.S. and internationally.
 

 Solo Travelers
 I know what it takes to navigate a place alone and make the most of it. Living in Japan for two years, I had to figure out travel, logistics, and daily life in a completely foreign environment—eventually planning my own solo pilgrimage along the Shikoku Henro. Later, as a filmmaker, I frequently found myself in new cities alone, creating self-guided itineraries to explore places like Nashville, TN, or San Francisco, CA. I understand the freedom, challenges, and opportunities that come with solo travel and can help others maximize their experience.
 

 Families with Young Kids
 As a father of five, I know firsthand how to balance adventure with practicality. My family lived full-time in a motorhome for two years, adapting our itinerary on the fly as we explored historical sites, national parks, and cities across the U.S. We’ve also traveled internationally, navigating England, France, Denmark, and Italy with kids in tow—learning how to craft the perfect mix of sightseeing, downtime, and immersive cultural experiences. I know how to build itineraries that keep kids engaged, avoid meltdowns, and make family travel rewarding for everyone.
 

 Multi-Generational Groups
 Traveling with multiple generations requires careful planning and flexibility. I’ve personally organized trips that included grandparents and even great-grandparents, ensuring experiences were accessible and enjoyable for all ages. Whether it was allowing younger members to take on a challenging hike while older members enjoyed a scenic alternative or adjusting pacing to accommodate different energy levels, I know how to structure trips that work for everyone.
 

 Travelers Aged 60+
 Through Vacation Races and Global Adventures, I frequently worked with travelers aged 60+, many of whom were retired couples looking for adventure. I’ve curated itineraries that balanced activity and accessibility, offering alternative experiences when needed. I understand how to craft travel plans that match different fitness levels while ensuring an enriching, engaging experience.
 

 First-Time Adventurers
 Helping first-time adventurers step into the outdoors is something I love. At Vacation Races, I often worked with participants who had never explored a national park before, helping them plan experiences that matched their comfort level. Leading youth backpacking and canyoneering trips, I’ve guided people through their first big outdoor challenges, helping them gain confidence and a love for adventure. I know how to remove barriers, set realistic expectations, and make sure new travelers feel prepared and excited rather than overwhelmed.
 

 Extreme Athletes
 At Vacation Races, I built events specifically designed for extreme athletes—ultramarathons, multi-day stage races, and endurance challenges that pushed limits. Beyond the events, I worked closely with athletes to recommend additional high-intensity outdoor experiences that matched their skill and stamina. I understand how to craft adventure-driven itineraries that cater to those looking for the toughest trails, biggest climbs, and most rewarding physical challenges.
 

 Whether it's a solo traveler looking for an immersive experience, a family trying to make the most of their time, or an extreme athlete searching for the next big challenge, I have the experience and insight to help craft unforgettable adventures.

Name: Katherine Jaeger
Profession: Professional guide and family travel expert
Short_Bio: Katherine has worked in environmental education for over 14 years, including seven as South Dakota’s Youth Outdoor Education Specialist. She's guided canoe and backpacking trips, taught hundreds of kids about the natural world, and helped people of all ages build confidence outdoors. She's a mom to three little ones under five—and they're out adventuring every chance they get. Whether you’re looking to plan a trip with kids, get your bearings in the Midwest or Plains states, or finally take that first step into camping or canoeing, she's excited to help you build something meaningful, accessible, and full of joy.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I have always loved recreating in the outdoors. I grew up fishing and hiking in the Midwest with long family roadtrips to National Parks each summer. I spent 14 years in environmental education and am passionate about getting youth outside. Now that I am a mom to 3 little ones, I love to explore alongside them.
Professional_expertise_raw: I spent 14 years in environmental education, including the last 7 as the Youth Outdoor Educational Specialist for the state of South Dakota. I'm a former canoe guide, have taken groups of kids backpacking and canoe camping, and am a WFR and lifeguard.
Travel_types_experience: In environmental education I aimed to make everyone comfortable with what we were doing. I also have worked with groups of youth for 14 + years, nd now have a 4 year old, 3 year old, and 1 year old of my own. We regularly adventure together.

 United states: Great plains: south dakota: black hills and badlands
 

 

 Familiar with about half of the national parks
Fav_outdoor_experiences: Taking 28 non English speaking students canoe camping in Voyageurs NP for a week. Backpacking Havasupai. Hiking in the Black Hills.

Name: Roger L. Semler
Short_Bio: Roger became a National Park Ranger in 1977, and I’ve spent the decades since serving as a steward of America’s wildest places—from the volcanic landscapes of Katmai to the backpacker mecca of Glacier National Park, to the vast Brooks Range of Gates of the Arctic. Over the years, he worked as the Chief Ranger of Gates of the Arctic, and first Leave No Coordinator in the country.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: During my youth, I was inspired by our national' wildlands and National Parks and dreamed of becoming a Park Ranger in the National Park Service (NPS). That dream came true in 1977 when I was hired as a seasonal Backcountry Ranger at Katmai National Monument, Alaska (now Katmai National Park and Preserve). My experiences at Katmai, and in particular the remarkable Valley of 10,000 Smokes, served as the source of my wilderness ethos and led a multi-decade career in the NPS in which I specialized in wilderness stewardship and management. In addition to Katmai, my NPS career spanned assignments at Hawaii Volcanoes National Park, Mount Rainier National Park, Glacier National Park, Gates of the Arctic National Park and Preserve, and the NPS Washington Office. Highlights of my career included serving as the NPS first Leave No Trace Program Coordinator and first -Wilderness Manager at Glacier National Park, recipient of the NPS Directors' Wilderness Star Award in 1998, service as the Chief Park Ranger at Gates of the Arctic National Park and Preserve, and the honor of serving as the NPS wilderness Stewardship Program Manager from 2014 until my retirement on February 28, 2025. I have extensive knowlede of the outdoor recreation and wilderness experience opportunities available in those NPS units.  I'm particularly interested in sharing my expertise in helping people plan outdoor reception opportunities and itineraries at Glacier National Park, Katmai National Park and Preserve, Gates of the Arctic National Park and Preserve.
Professional_expertise_raw: I have extensive experience as a National Park Service Ranger with a strong body of work involving wilderness search and rescue; wilderness safety, survival and travel; Leave No Trace outdoor ethics and wilderness education.
Travel_types_experience: As a National Park Service Ranger for multiple decades, I have served, informed, consulted with, and advised all of the categories identified above.

Katmai National Park and Preserve, Alaska

 - Valley of 10,000 Smokes
 

 Gates of the Arctic National Park and Preserve, Alaska

 - Arrigetch Peaks
 - Anaktuvuk Pass
 

 Glacier National Park, Montana
 - all backcountry areas
Fav_outdoor_experiences:
1) I have an annual tradition of engaging in a multi-day solo backcountry ski trip into the backcountry of Glacier National Park.
 2) I frequently engage in wilderness treks into Katmai's Valley of 10,000 Smokes every other year. all told, I have spent of 150 days in the "Valley" since 1977.
 3) My favorite arctic river float trip is the Noatak River in Gates of eh Arctic National Park and Preserve. This is a quintessential l arctic river experience.

Local draft beer

Name: Carlos Martinez Abad
Short_Bio:
Carlos was born and raised in Valencia, Spain and is now based on the beautiful island of Mallorca. He's spent over 20 years designing and leading exclusive biking and hiking adventures across Spain—specializing in immersive, active journeys that go beyond the usual stops.

As co-founder of The Ventourist, he creates custom experiences that connect travelers with Spain’s landscapes, food, and rhythm
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I was born and raised in Valencia, Spain, but I've been living for many years on the incredible island of Mallorca. Biking and hiking are my main passions, and I’ve spent over 20 years designing and leading exclusive active adventures across several countries, with a special focus on Spain. I love exploring off-the-beaten-path locations and sharing Spain’s hidden gems—especially on two wheels—with those who seek unique and unforgettable experiences.
Professional_expertise_raw: I’ve worked for several companies in the active travel sector for years. Now, I co-own and run The Ventourist, crafting select active experiences in Spain and beyond.
Travel_types_experience: My specialty is designing personalized trips tailored to different ages, activity levels, and preferences. I’m confident in providing expert advice to travelers in any of these categories, as long as it’s a destination I know well.
Geographic_areas_raw: Spain: Mallorca, Catalunya, Andalucia, Galicia, Basque Coutry, La Rioja, Valencia, Gran Canaria and beyond
Fav_outdoor_experiences: Road biking in Mallorca. Hiking in the Pyrenees. Wine tasting in La Rioja
Other_hobbies_interests: I’m a big foodie—I love discovering new restaurants and trying local dishes wherever I go. I also meditate daily and have a passion for learning languages—it's something I never get tired of =)

Spanish & Catalan - Native
 German, French, Italian - Fluent

Name: Katie Davis
Short_Bio: Katie has been skiing since she could walk. She's an avid solo traveler with over 30 countries under her belt (and counting). She's planned everything from vanlife trips through the fjords of Norway to multi-stop family ski vacations.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: Skiing has been the center of my universe practically since I was born. Put on a pair of sticks at a year and a half old, and my destiny was set. I moved to Boulder, CO after graduating high school and knew I wanted to work within the ski world, I just had absolutely no idea how. I learned about the outdoor industry, and my passions and career goals intertwined from there on. I additionally am an avid solo female traveler, visiting over 30 countries mostly solo. I aim to make solo female travel less scary for others one story at a time.
Professional_expertise_raw: I work in the PR sphere of the outdoor industry, with a preference for adventure travel or ski based clients. I additionally worked with a charter yacht company and hold an STCW certification to work on yachts, so I am familiar with water-based trips as well. I haven’t designed trips outside myself, friends and family, but after solo traveling for extended periods of time, I know how to plan a trip to the most minute of details.
Travel_types_experience:
I am a solo traveler, so I know precisely what solo travelers think like and want to plan like (allowing input and different travel styles and budget of course). 
 My friends and family are not as well traveled, and often as first-time tips to a region or country, which I can provide. 
 In my prior work in the outdoor industry, I have worked with numerous pro skiers and know which questions to ask to help them plan their best adventure. 
 My own family consists of many skill sets, which I always consider while planning family trips to ski destinations.
Geographic_areas_raw:
Faroe Islands: Tórshavn, Sandøy
 Norway: Oslo, the mountains/fjords between Bergen and Alesund, Lofoten Islands, Tromsø
 Austria: Vienna, Innsbruck, St. Anton am Alberg
 Ireland: Dublin, Galway, Belfast
 Switzerland: Zermatt, Verbier, Le Chable, Geneva
Fav_outdoor_experiences: My favorite trip was to Norway, and the times I have traveled there since. In 2022 I rented a car to sleep in and drive around in between Trondheim and Tromsø for 14 days. I hiked, horseback rode, drove and ate my way through one of the most beautiful places in the world. The following year, I did the same over a week between Bergen and Alesund. While historic storms waylaid many of my plans, being in a van always meant I was on the move, outside in the sunshine (in its fleeting moments) and ready to explore the beautiful small communities among the fjords.
Languages_raw: I do not. I speak very very little German, French and Spanish (enough to provide helpful phrases while traveling, but not more than that)

Name: Kimberly Ann Hagan
Short_Bio: Kim bring decades of experience guiding and designing travel around the world—as an active travel guide leading hiking and biking trips across Europe, working at a scuba dive shop in Indonesia, and an international meetings and events planner. she's led travelers through Tuscany’s hill towns, the Swiss Alps’ hidden trails, Ireland’s coastal peninsulas, and Australia’s rainforests—and helped first-time adventurers feel at home around the world.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I love traveling and exploring the world! I started traveling when I graduated from collage and have not really stopped! I love exploring nature and the outdoors around the world. It is there that I find myself most connected with the beauty and the essence of myself and life!
Professional_expertise_raw:
My best friend's small daughter once asked her if I was "an Artist or an Adventure?" I love those titles and like to think of myself as both! I have worked in the travel industry for 30 years as an adventure travel guide for Backroads for 11 years leading hiking, biking and multi-sport trips, at a scuba diving shop in Indonesia for 5 years, and then in the meetings and events world internationally for the last 15 years. I have experience on everything from camping road trips to staying at luxury hotels and everything in-between! 
 

 As a side note, I also have a Masters in Nutrition from Sheffield University and have studied a lot of yoga and other wellness and healing modalities.
Travel_types_experience:
I started traveling as a solo traveler when I was 23 and over 30 years later, I am still doing it! Solo travel is my favorite way to travel and it is what I know best! Additionally, many of the people who visited the dive shop in Indonesia were solo travelers, so this is a group I feel so intuitively connected to! I LOVE encouraging people to travel on their own as I feel you learn so much more about a place, people, and yourself when you travel on your own. It is such an opportunity for personal growth and your own human development! 
 I led solo, multi-generational, and family group trips at Backroads for 11 years, so I have lots of experience with all there of these, probably the multigenerational being my favorite out of the 3 as I like diverse groups and feel like it adds so much more perspective and insight to travel experience.

Name: Haley Jurena
Short_Bio:
Haley's love of wild places began with a road trip to 8 national parks—and turned into a career in conservation, guiding, and outdoor travel. She worked as a forester in Sequoia and Hawai‘i, then spent years as a Park Ranger in Grand Teton and Rocky Mountain National Parks. She's advised thousands of travelers on everything from backcountry permits to scenic drives to ranger-led experiences.

After her time in the parks, I shifted into travel planning—specializing in hiking and biking trips across the UK and Ireland, and now coordinating operations for journeys across the American Southwest. She's backpacked the Dolomites, motorbiked the Ha Giang Loop, and dove the Great Barrier Reef.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: After a month long road trip to 8 National Parks out west, I knew I wanted to dedicate my life to protecting those spaces. I graduated with a degree in Environmental Science and went on to work as a Park Ranger for National Parks such as Grand Teton and Rocky Mountain. Now working in the travel industry, I’ve had the amazing opportunity to explore many outdoor playgrounds such as the Dolomites, Japan, Australia, Thailand, and more!
Professional_expertise_raw: Starting out in the conservation industry, I worked as a forester in Sequoia National Forest and on O’ahu, Hawai’i. I wanted to connect more to people and the outdoors and became a Park Ranger at Rocky Mountain and Grand Teton National Parks for 2 years. I then became an adventure travel sales specialist where my focus was on the UK and Ireland (think West Highland Way, Dingle Way, Cotswolds, etc). I am currently an Operations Coordinator at another travel company where I focus on the Southwest US (New Mexico, Arizona, Texas, etc).
Travel_types_experience: As a park ranger, I advised all types of folks on safe and memorable travel experiences in the parks ranging from driving tours, multi day backpacking trips, and accessible day hikes. These included families, solo travelers, as well as seasoned and first time travelers to the National Parks. As an adventure travel sales specialist and operations coordinator, I work mostly with folks 60+, advising on incredible hiking and biking opportunities that fit their desires, needs, and mobility.
Geographic_areas_raw:
Italy: Dolomites: Cortina d’Amprezzo: AV1 Trail
 United States: Colorado: Estes Park: Rocky Mountain National Park
 United States: Wyoming: Jackson Hole: Grand Tetons
 United States: Hawaiian Islands: Oahu & Maui
 Japan: Tokyo & Kyoto

 United States: Utah: Springdale: Zion National Park
 United States: New Mexico: Santa Fe

 United States: Wisconsin: Whole State
 United States: Minnesota: Whole State
Fav_outdoor_experiences: Diving the Great Barrier Reef in Cairns changed my life- seeing 4 foot clams and pristine coral was out of this world. Backpacking in the Dolomites sparked such a sense of awe and sparked a desire to see more of the world in two feet. Motorbiking the Ha Giang Loop in Vietnam was exhilarating and pushed me to my limits, resulting in some of the most breathtaking views of my life.
Other_hobbies_interests: I love tapping into some creative activities like sewing and drawing. I am definitely less talented in singing, but karaoke lights me up!

Name: Dino Katalenic
Short_Bio: Dino spent the past 15+ years designing and leading itineraries across Europe, South America, and southern Africa, blending world-class dining and active adventure with immersive cultural experiences. Originally from Slovenia and Croatia,he's worked with global travel companies like Backroads as well as boutique agencies, helping clients discover everything from Patagonia’s remote trails to the best late-night tapas in Andalusia.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: Ex-Backroads, now I consult hospitality businesses on how to be better in the ever changing world of luxury tourism. I am still a part time guide for various companies and loooove the outdoors and spend any and every free time out there when I can.
Professional_expertise_raw: 11 years with Backroads, 3 years with Iconic and private clients along the way. Helped create multiple itineraries (either for corporate group travel or personal itineraries). During covid I helped build and start a small boutique hotel with a fine dining restaurant, which gained 2 michelin stars in less than 2 years. I am at home in tourism and active travel.
Travel_types_experience: I mainly cater to adults who love the outdoors. I am not comfortable advising to young kids, because I do now know what ticks their boxes, but everything else I am comfortable with and can adapt to the guest’s need. With extreme athletes I have connections I can put them with, to create a truly bespoke experience.

I love the Mediterranean with the combination of mountains and the sea (Slovenia, Dolomites, Croatia, Basque Country, Catalonia, South of France) where you have a myriad of outdoors options as well as great history and culture, amazing food and wine and one of the best ways to live your life with nature.

Name: Steve Engelhardt
Short_Bio: Steve was raised in Rocky Mountain National Park and spent much of his life hunting, fishing, and learning the rhythms of the mountains. After a corporate career, he followed my lifelong passion and joined the National Park Service, serving as a ranger in parks from Alaska to Maine
What_drew_you_to_outdoors:
Raised in Rocky Mountain National Park. 
 Lived most of my life in Colorado and hunted and fished extensively in the Mountains. Volunteered as a field wildlife researcher for Colorado Game and Fish. 
 After retirement from the corporate world I joined the National Ark Service and was a Ranger in Rocky Mountain, Organ Pipe , Denali, Montezumas Castle/Tuzigoot, North Cascades ( two years with one on east side and one on West side) Acadia (Schoodic specialist) and Sequoia/Kings Canyon 
 Traveled to over 50 countries and been to many major sites like Machu Pichu, Tikal, the Kremlin and St Petersburgs Hermitage. Very extensive European travel
Professional_expertise_raw: Along with the experience I listed the the previous box I have also been a volunteer wildlife researcher for Arizona Game and Fish and a Arizona State Parks volunteer program presenter
Travel_types_experience: As a Ranger I dealt with everyone. I like to educate so working with first timers is great. I am a senior so I clearly understand limitations and needs.
Geographic_areas_raw: Much of the Germanic regions of Europe, Rocky Mountain National Park. Denali, North Cascades NP, Montezumas Castle/Tuzigoot Area , Organ Pipe Cactus. Schoodic Pennisula of Acadia NP, Cedar Grove in Kings Canyon NP, Lost Dutchman State Park, Roosevelt National Forest and entire area around Estes Park (home to Rocky), a great deal of Europe.
Fav_outdoor_experiences:
At the foot of Denali I came within feet of a huge blank wolf with a caribou bone in its mouth. Sitting on a rock outcropping over several meadows watching for elk. 
 Climbing vertical cliffs on mountains and the incredible joy you feel upon pulling yourself over the top. Watching monster storm waves crash into the rocks on Acadias Maine Coast
Other_hobbies_interests:
I am very involved in giving business and outdoor seminars and workshops. I love public speaking and do it two or more times a week. 
 I like to think that I am sort of a historian in several areas
Languages_raw: Sorry. But I leave languages to my wife.

Name: Dakota Walz
Short_Bio: Dakota is a  climber, wilderness EMT, and solo adventurer with experience across Jordan, Chad, Thailand, Mexico, Greece, and the U.S. While much of his time is spent on big climbs and remote expeditions, he also loves helping people navigate more approachable hikes, scenic routes, and cultural experiences—especially in Thailand, where he spent months exploring by motorbike.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: Since I was a child the outdoors have been a source of curiosity and wonder for me. Being outside in real life gives me peace and balance, providing an escape from daily stress. It keeps me active and connected to nature, which plays a central role in my life.
Professional_expertise_raw: I am a professional adventurer that travels the world establishing new rock climbs for both serious athletes and noobs alike. I am also a wilderness and urban EMT with have 7 years experience providing care in a 911 capacity including abroad. Additionally, I am a volunteer data collector working with biologists at the Peregrine Fund in the remote tundra of Alaska.
Travel_types_experience:
Though often my expeditions include a full team, I also have extensive experience traveling solo in the US and abroad. Much of my travel is in the name of extreme adventures such as rock climbing, motorcycling, pack-rafting, trapeze, etc. 
 

 When stateside I enjoy taking the time to answer questions folks have about my trips and feed their curiosity in hopes to inspire them to one day take the plunge into their own adventures. I remember what it feels like to be a first-time adventurer because I try to always be doing new things for the first time!
Geographic_areas_raw: Ennedi, nearly all, Wadi Rum, El Potrero Chico, Manikia
Fav_outdoor_experiences:
My favorite trip? The next one. 
 

 My second favorite trip? After 3 months of solo traveling Thailand, I met and fell in love with another solo traveler. We spent a week riding motorbikes, exploring caves, and observing bat migrations deep in the northern ranges along the border of Myanmar. 
 

 After that week together, when my return flight to the US was about to leave, she asked me to skip it and continue traveling with her into Laos, Cambodia, and Vietnam for two more months. That was 2 years ago and the magic is still so strong I feel like the trip never ended.

Name: Laura Scharle
Short_Bio: Laura has spent her life by the water—first as a marine science-obsessed kid in the Chesapeake Bay region, then as a guide and park ranger leading kayak tours, beach walks, and outdoor festivals across Delaware’s seashore parks. For 17 years, she helped visitors of all backgrounds connect with nature through recreation, interpretation, and storytelling. Today, she runs Delmarva Trails and Waterways, a resource I built to connect people with the outdoors through real, local knowledge.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I was drawn to marine science as a career path starting in the first grade. Obsessed with learning about the Chesapeake Bay ecosystem, I got a degree in Ecology. I went on to work as a naturalist and outdoor guide for Delaware State Parks, and eventually became manager of all interpretive programs in the seashore region of Delaware. I recently completed a lifetime goal of kayaking around the whole Delmarva Peninsula (about 600 miles!). My husband is an avid kayak fisherman and we are raising a son with special needs where we are seeing the benefits of being outdoors firsthand in a child's development. From speech and language to gross and fine motor skills, the outdoors has been crucial for his development.
Professional_expertise_raw:
As mentioned in the previous question, I worked for Delaware State Parks for 17 years. There is managed a team of 10, and oversaw all interpretive and recreational programs in the seashore region of the state. Guided hikes, kayak tours, historic reenactments, surf fishing classes, beach driving clinics, and all kinds of outdoor special events. 
 

 I left that position about 2 years ago to go out on my own as an independent contractor. I do marketing for a variety of outdoor recreation entities and run my own outdoor travel website, Delmarva trails and waterways.com, which connect people to all trails, kayak launches, beaches, and piers on the Delmarva Peninsula.
Travel_types_experience: I get inquiries through my website ALL the time from all kinds of people. They are typically looking for places to paddle, kayak-camp, fish, and just relax on a beach. Most are retirees, but I've also advised homeschool groups, young families, and paddling/hiking clubs.
Geographic_areas_raw:
United States: Delaware (South of the C&D canal)
 United States: Maryland (eastern shore)
 United States: Virginia (eastern shore)
Fav_outdoor_experiences:
Briefly? That's tough! But I'd have to say kayak-camping. My family and I love paddling to a remote beach and setting up camp on an island for the weekend. Our son was even conceived on a kayak camping trip! 
 I'm also very proud of my accomplishment in paddling around the entire Delmarva Peninsula. I did it in little 8-12 mile chunks over the course of 11 years, but I did it. It's something I started before I had a kid, before I got married, and even had a career. It was the one constant in my life when things were changing. It was a form of self care that was always available to me when I was stressed or feeling down. It got me through some incredibly tough times in me life. I also learned a lot about sea kayaking skills and safety along the way, taking ACA courses, learning self rescues, on-water navigation, and so much more. My next goal is to paddle the length of all the major rivers on the peninsula.
Other_hobbies_interests:
I enjoy playing piano and guitar. It's a hobby that was put on the back burner after college that I have recently gotten back into and I'm loving it.
 I also love to travel. I did a lot of international travel when I was younger, and now I love road trips with my son. He is only 9 but he's already been to 17 states and we hope to log more this summer.
 I also love maps. I create custom trail maps for some of my clients, I plan itineraries with Google maps, and my son is obsessed with maps and Google earth.
Languages_raw: I can get by in broken Spanish, but far from fluent.

Name: Megan Stump
Short_Bio: Megan spent 11 years at REI, working in retail, guiding trips, and coordinating operations for the Experiences division. Before that, she worked in natural resources management with various land agencies
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: Spending time outside has a way of grounding me — it clears the noise, requires me to slow down, and helps me breathe a little easier. It’s the medicine I can bottle up and take with me to the “real world.”
Professional_expertise_raw: I previously worked for REI for 11 years, both in Retail and in the now defunct Experiences division as an Instructor, Guide, and Operations Coordinator. Prior to REI, I worked in natural resources management for various land management agencies and have an extensive understanding of public lands recreation and policy.
Travel_types_experience: I have experience working with each of these travel types, but feel my strengths lie primarily with empowering folks to reach for an experience they might otherwise think is unattainable for them.

Name: Dani Reyes-Acosta
Profession: Mountain athlete, filmmaker, and outdoor Educator
Short_Bio: Dani is a splitboarder, climber, mountain runner, and storyteller. My journey took me from a cushy corporate career to ticking off climbs and ski lines across the Americas, all while rediscovering my roots as a food producer and cultivator.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors:
Based in SW Colorado, I proudly reclaim the complexities of my heritage as a food producer, cultivator, and legacy Coloradan. My goal as a splitboarder, climber, and mountain runner (plus storyteller) is to find the limits of my possible, searching out the peaks and valleys that tell the story of Mother Earth's most impacted places. In community with ourselves and the world at large, we'll unEarth the power within—to form a better future for us all.
 

 As a four-season mountain athlete and storyteller, I aim to inspire individual action and collective communion through stories of self-care and self-determination found in the "great outdoors." After leaving cushy corporate life to find my way back to my roots, I ticked rock climbs and ski lines across Argentina, Chile, Canada, and the USA on a circuitous path to self-actualization.
 

 The outdoors have helped me find who I am - and who I want to be - and I hope to share this parallel path with others!
Professional_expertise_raw:
I am a narrative strategist connecting next-gen leaders to themselves and Mother Nature. As a professional mountain athlete, visual storyteller, and strategic advisor, I layer my marketer and design background with a calling to adventure and advocate for a just transition and "everyone outside."
 

 My work empowers individual and collective action, advocates for climate justice, and rewrites the narrative of how we build community on this planet.
 

 As a professional mountain athlete and storyteller, I explore the intersections of humans with nature, focusing on cultural inquiry and reciprocity. I am also a filmmaker, creative producer, and writer who creates impactful branded and documentary content. Finally, as a public speaker, panelist, facilitator, and workshop leader, I engage diverse audiences on topics like outdoor leadership, social entrepreneurship, and authenticity. 
 

 I am a trilingual (English, Spanish, and French) and multicultural (Mestiza and Filipina) storyteller who leverages my unique perspective and voice to inspire and challenge others in their own journeys of collective and creative connection.
 

 If you really want to nerd out with me, please see this pitch deck on my varied lanes of work: https://bit.ly/HolaDRA
Travel_types_experience: I have the lived experience of identifying with all of these traveler types!

Name: Billy Strasser

Short_Bio:
Billy has worked as a National Park Service ranger and educator across more than 12 park sites, helping people connect with nature and the stories of place.

Most recently, he retired from New River Gorge National Park in West Virginia.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I grew up on a farm, and as a child wandered the fields looking for arrowheads. It was probably inevitable that I combined my love of the outdoors with my love of history, and spent most of my life working in the national parks.
Professional_expertise_raw: I've worked as a Park Ranger (Interpretation) in over 12 US National Park sites. I worked as a front-line interpreter, connecting people with the outdoors. I am currently retired from New River Gorge National Park in WV.
Travel_types_experience: In my work as a park ranger, have helped people of all backgrounds from all over the world plan trips to their national parks.
