Name: Marcy Tillman
Short_Bio:
Marcy has hiked hundreds of miles around Chattanooga and Northwest Georgia. 
She  volunteers as a docent at Lula Lake Land Trust, and loves helping others—especially women and families—feel more comfortable, capable, and inspired in wild places.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I grew up in rural West Virginia, surrounded by the Appalachian Mountains. However, it wasn't until becoming a student at Virginia Tech in 1996 that I began to truly appreciate the outdoors. Though I dabbled in hiking throughout the 20 years that followed - mostly during vacations to places like the Smoky Mountains, Colorado, and Washington state - my deep love for hiking settled in 2021 following my divorce. At that time, mental health was becoming a major factor in my world as I processed this new complex season of life as a working single mom. On weekends that my children had visitation with their dad, I rekindled my love for walking, and eventually started setting off on hiking trails in the Chattanooga area. I was meeting amazing people out on trails, many of whom recommended other trails that I would then be encouraged to try next. Exploring the outdoors on a trail quickly became a lifeline for me, not only improving my physical health, but my emotional health as well. In addition to the exposure to tremendous beauty, I started to see what I was capable of as I pushed myself to try challenges and navigate paths, boulder fields, and increase my stamina. In April 2022, I set out to complete my longest day hike yet: the 12-mile mixed terrain of the Fiery Gizzard Trail in Grundy County, TN. Since 2021, I have hiked several hundred miles of day hikes in the greater Chattanooga area, exploring trails and beauty I didn't realize existed for so many years: Lula Lake Land Trust, Cloudland Canyon State Park, Savage Gulf, Edward's Point, Sunset Rock, and more. I have also explored some new trails, including Camelback Mountain in Phoenix, AZ, and NC trails such as Hanging Rock State Park. I would be honored to share my trail experiences, tips, and suggestions with others - especially single women or moms hiking with children - who are interested in exploring hiking trails.
Professional_expertise_raw: I am currently serving as a Docent Volunteer at Lula Lake Land Trust on Lookout Mountain, GA. LLLT is a private land trust open to the public (via reservation) on the first and last weekend of each month. As a docent volunteer, I am stationed at a table near the lake to provide information to visitors, including the history of LLLT, guidance regarding hiking trail suggestions, and directions around the Core Reserve property. I also assist with special events, such as the Hike, Bike, and Brew festival.
Travel_types_experience: As stated in my previous responses, I am a single mom and have mostly hiked solo. I have also explored trails with my children when they were as young as 4 years old and as old as 14 years old. Having family members of a variety of ages, I aim to objectively match difficulty level with skill level to make sure each person has the maximum experience while not being pushed beyond their individual ability.
Geographic_areas_raw:
- United States: Northwest Georgia: Cloudland Canyon State Park (Sitton's Gulch Trail, Rim Trail), Lula Lake Land Trust (all trails), Chickamauga National Battlefield
 - United States: Greater Chattanooga, TN: Lookout Mountain: Sunset Rock, Point Park, Guild Trail 
 - United States: Greater Chattanooga, TN: Downtown and Signal Mountain: Stringer's Ridge, Rainbow Lake, Signal Point, Edward's Point, North Chickamauga State Park
 - United States: TN: Grundy County: Fiery Gizzard, Foster Falls, Denny Cove
 - United States: TN: Grundy/Sequatchie Counties: Savage Gulf: Savage Falls, Great Stone Door Trail, Savage Day Loop, Suter Falls, Horsepound Falls
 

 Random addition (not really hiking, but still know like the back of my hand): United States: South Carolina: Fripp Island
Fav_outdoor_experiences:
If you see me on a trail, I am likely to say, "This is one of my favorites!" no matter where I am, because there is always beauty to see, hear, and explore - and also strength, confidence, and relationships that come from those challenges! I have met so many amazing people. I have seen so many amazing sights. It's hard to pick true favorites.
 

 Lula Lake will always hold a special place in my heart. I call it the Disney World of hiking. Every time I go, I try to mix and match the trail combination to make sure I enjoy both the bluff view that overlooks Chattanooga Valley as well as take in the gorgeous 120-foot waterfall or the peaceful lake. My favorite time there was actually February of 2023, when I visited following an intense rain/freeze. The spray from the falls had created an icy, frozen wonderland in the space closest to the falls, and the fullness of the falls was awe-inspiring. 
 

 Another one of my favorite experiences was hiking Camelback Mountain in Phoenix in December of 2023. Having spent all of my life in the Mid-Atlantic region and the southern US, I had never been on a hike in such rugged, wild terrain. Also, we went at Christmas time, and Santa was at the summit! lol
Other_hobbies_interests:
I am a severe weather enthusiast and am a YouTube moderator for a storm chaser.
 

 Also, my degree is in music, so I have a very deep appreciation for music of all genres.
 

 As I mentioned before, I am a mental health advocate.
 

 And I love my kiddos! I have four children, ages 17, 14, 11, and 7.

Name: Magdalene Blakeson
Short_Bio: Magdalene  grew up in Vermont, where she discovered the joy and adventure of being outdoors. She spent over a decade guiding in national parks, Arctic wildernesses, and remote corners of Scandinavia—and now run a small company offering dog sledding, trekking, and hut-to-hut skiing adventures in Norway and beyond.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors:
I consider myself incredibly lucky to have grown up in beautiful Vermont, surrounded by a community that deeply valued its connection to nature. From a young age, I’ve been fascinated by the natural world, spending as much time as possible outdoors. It’s where I feel most alive and connected to everything around me. This love for nature has remained at the core of my life.
 

 For over 10 years, I’ve had the privilege of working in the outdoor industry as a guide, an expedition leader, and now as the owner of a small company. Through all these roles, one thing has remained constant: my passion for creating experiences that allow people to reconnect with nature. I thrive on offering the kind of challenges and thrills that come from a true adventure, and I find it incredibly rewarding to help others rediscover a simpler way of living and the joy that comes from being immersed in the natural world.
Professional_expertise_raw:
I work primiarily as a guide in Europe and the United States. I live in Norway and guide all over the country. I have also guided all over the U.S. in many of the national parks and spent several years in Alaska. I also have expereince guiding and living in Polar regions (Alaska, northern Canada, northern Norway and Svalbard in the high Arctic). I am well connected to communities and love designing unique adventures. My main areas of expertise are Dog Sledding, Trekking, and hut to hut skiing. 
 My company designs trips and offers tours in Scandinavia and will be extending to Vermont and Alaska.
Travel_types_experience: In my guiding work I have worked with solo-travellers and people of all ages from 18-83. I have enough experience to understand varying levels of fitness and comfort and can reccomend trips based on the needs of the client.
Geographic_areas_raw:
Norway: Northern, Southwestern Norway, Lofoten Islands.
 Svalbard: high arctic adventures (dog sled, snowmobile, northern lights, ice cave)
 Sweden: Northern Sweden, Midwest Sweden. Kebnekaise, Kungsleden and Abisko. 
 United States: Alaska-Juneau, Anchorage area, Fairbanks, Dogsledding and northen lights adventures 
 United States: Vermont- all over and fall foliage 
 United States: New Hampshire- White mountains
 United States: Grand Canyon
Fav_outdoor_experiences: It is hard to choose favorite as there are so many! Exploring the ice caves and venturing into the vast wilderness of Svalbard by dog team have been unforgettable experiences. It’s truly one of the most stunning places on Earth—the ever-changing weather and chance to spot polar bears only adds to the thrill. Equally magical has been hiking above the fjords of southwestern Norway, where the land’s beauty and serenity are beyond compare. And of course, trekking through Vermont in the fall is always a highlight—the breathtaking reds, oranges, and yellows of the foliage, combined with the cozy sweater weather and fresh apple cider donuts make it a special kind of magic.
Other_hobbies_interests:
Pottery- working with clay 
 Knitting
 Skijouring- being pulled by huskies 
 Baking

Name: Nichole Sellon
Short_Bio:
Nichole is trail runner, bike tourer, yoga teacher, writer, and mountain mover. I’ve spent the past decade chasing trails across the U.S. and the world, from Colorado’s 13ers to Central America’s volcanoes.She's run ultras, fastpacked through the Cascades, and pedaled thousands of miles between summits.

Today, she lives in Colorado, where she run year-round and work as a writer and trail editor for Komoot and AllTrails
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I started trail running in 2010 and have never looked back. It is my first love and passion, and searching for new backcountry has dictated where I have chosen to live in the United States since then. It has also dictated how I travel internationally—always going to places with trails and mountains or choosing to run and walk everywhere to find adventure. I found my forever home base in Colorado four years ago because it has a mountain trail network that stays snow-free year round (which means I can run all year), and I am 20 minutes from several backcountry trailheads that travel deep into two different mountain ranges.
Professional_expertise_raw:
I have competitively run ultra marathons, running all distances up to 100 kilometers, and have paced and crewed several friends in the hundred-mile distance. These races took me all over the Cascades of Washington and Oregon. I have also backpacked and fastpacked hundreds of miles into the backcountry. Living in Colorado, I have climbed several 14ers and 13ers and continue to check new ones off the list every summer. I have biked thousands of miles on bike tours around the United States. 
 

 I am a certified personal trainer, health coach, yoga instructor, and EMT in a rural environment. I was WFR certified but have since let it expire.
 

 Over the last two years, I have worked as an editor, writer, and auditor for Komoot and AllTrails, extensively researching trails all over the United States and creating routes for people to safely enjoy the outdoors.
Travel_types_experience:
I have spent most of my time traveling alone, domestically and internationally. I have direct advice about safe solo travel as a female in South and Central America and Europe. This is also specific to getting outside safely as a solo female international traveler, as I have hiked many trails and run around cities alone. I spent several summers living out of my truck alone at trailheads all over the West.
 

 I remember being a first-time traveler or adventurer and all the things I wished I had known a little earlier or before I set out. I can put myself in the shoes of those new to a sport or a place. I still find myself in the "newbie" spot since I consistently travel, try new things, and run in new places. 
 

 I can advise any extreme trail runners, bike tourists, or backpackers. I have over ten years of running long distances and several days in the backcountry of the mountains. I have experienced injuries, GI and diet issues, inclement weather, animal encounters, and overall fatigue.
Geographic_areas_raw:
United States: 
 Washington: Cougar Mountain, Tiger Mountain, North Cascades, Olympic Peninsula, The Enchantments, The Wonderland Trail (Mt. Rainier, 2 days), The Loowit Trail (Mt. Saint Helens, fastpack and ultra-marathon race), Stehekin (4-day backpack trip), Seattle 
 Oregon: Timberline Trail (Mt Hood Circumnavigation, 9 hours), Mt. Hood Summit climb, Three Sisters circumnavigation, South Sister summit climb, The Wallowas, The Columbia River Gorge. Portland: Forest Park
 Colorado: The Gore Range, the Ten Mile Range, and the mountains around Breckenridge, Silverthorne, Frisco, Fairplay, and Alma. Colorado Trail. Mt. Elbert and Mt. Massive. Sawatch and Sangre de Cristo Mountain Ranges. Methodist Mountain and the Arkansas Hills Trail System. Pikes Peak. The Incline (Manitou Springs).  
 New Mexico: Taos, Santa Fe, Organ Mountains
 Arizona: Grand Canyon, Rim2R2R.
 California: Mission Trails Regional Park, Death Valley
 New York: Adirondack Mountains 
 Montana: Glacier National Park (west)
 

 International:
 Nepal: Everest Base Camp Trek
 Nicaragua: Ometepe Island 
 Belize: Caye Caulker (ran every street)
 Mexico: Todos Santos, Sayulita, Punta Mita
 Panama: Bocas del Toro
 Peru: Pisac, Cusco (Sacred Valley)
 Portugal: The Algarve, Lisbon
Fav_outdoor_experiences:
My best trail memories are:
 I ran the inaugural ultra race around Mount Saint Helens on the Loowit Trail and was in first place until I missed the last turn and got a bit lost. I ended up second female. This is, by far, the most beautiful and challenging trail I have run in the US. It is absolutely rugged and everything you could ask for from a backcountry experience. 
 The fastpack on the Wonderland Trail around Mount Rainier that a friend and I accomplished in two nights. We had the best weather you could possibly get in September in Washington. 
 My two-week bike tour from Seattle to Glacier National Park is still one of my favorite adventures, and I have been trying to plan an extensive amount of time off to go on another tour here in Colorado this summer, this time on a gravel bike. 
 On my recent trip to Ometepe Island, I summited Volcan Concepción and Volcan Maderas, and according to my guide, I had the fastest known tourist time up Concepción. . .
Other_hobbies_interests: Movement is my passion, and I spend a lot of time strength training and practicing yoga and breathwork. I regularly practice Spanish and guitar, and I enjoy reading. I enjoy hanging out with my friends outside, at the hot springs, or at a brewery. I love dogs, specifically border collies, but cannot yet have one of my own, so I adopt all my friend's dogs and take them running with me.
Languages_raw: I am only fluent in English.

Name: Abby Burlingame
Short_Bio: Abby grew up in the Seattle area, hiking and camping the Cascades in the summer and skiing them in the winter. She worked for a decade with the Washington Trails Association, followed by  a decade-long career with the National Park Service.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I grew up in the Seattle area, hiking and camping the Cascades in the summer and skiing them in the winter, but I drifted away from being outdoors in high school and college. Halfway through college, I felt the strong need to be outside exploring again, and after a summer spent as a Volunteer Vacation Crew Leader with Washington Trails Association, I got an internship with John Day Fossil Beds National Monument. That started almost a decade long career as a Park Ranger with the National Park Service, where being outdoors in some capacity was always part of my day. For me ‘outdoors’ has a broad definition--yes, it can be epic, once in a lifetime adventures in far away places, but it can also be a slow walk in local park, noticing things you’ve never noticed before. I’m passionate about the outdoors as a way to understand deeper, and love finding ways to connect people to place in meaningful ways.
Professional_expertise_raw: The bulk of my professional experience comes from working as a Park Ranger with Interpretation and Education. I’ve worked at smaller monuments like John Day Fossil Beds National Monument and Lava Beds National Monument, as well as larger parks like Kenai Fjords National Park and Carlsbad Caverns National Park, plus places like Lake Roosevelt National Recreation Area. My job duties in all these positions looked different, but in every job I provided advice and assistance to visitors on planning their trips both within the park, but also within the region at large.
Travel_types_experience: In my positions with the National Park Service, I’m used to giving advice and assistance to large family groups trying to meet the interests and needs of a wide variety of individuals from young kids to grandparents. I have a particular affinity for working with solo travelers, since I usually travel by myself. I enjoy giving advice and encouragement to those traveling alone, such as calming a nervous visitor who’s called the visitor center ahead of time to double check things. And then I’ve definitely worked both in educational programs within the National Park Service as well as with Washington Trails Association with folks who are spending their first significant amount of time outside, walking them on the ground through new skills and giving encouragement where needed.
Geographic_areas_raw:
United States: Washington: Whole State
 United States: Washington: Northern Idaho
 Canada: British Columbia: Vancouver, Victoria, Alaska Highway
 United States; Alaska: Seward Peninsula: Kenai Fjords National Park
 United States: New Mexico: Carlsbad
 United States: Oregon: Eastern Oregon: Bend, Crater Lake, Klamath Falls, John Day Fossil Beds
 United States: California: Northern California: Lassen, Mount Shasta, Lava Beds National Monument
Fav_outdoor_experiences: There’s almost too many to choose from! One of the toughest things I’ve done, which made it all the more memorable, was climb South Sister in the Cascades in Oregon. It’s a non-technical ascent, but as a stratovolcano, the last four miles or so of the trail to the top were all loose talus–all lightweight, red rocks that shifted around under our feet. It was very much three steps forward, and one (or even two!) steps back. Mentally one of the hardest sections of trail I’ve ever had to push myself through, but absolutely worth it at the top! I have also really enjoyed the time I’ve spent doing some thru hiking in the British Isles, in particular the Southwest Coastal Path, which was my first long hike over there. I only did a section, but it stood out because it was such a different way of being outside. I wasn’t ever far from civilization, which had its own charms, but climbing up and down and up and down the valley along the coast was its own challenge. Finally, my favorite is probably the trip that first prompted me down a career outdoors. My dad and I backpacked through North Cascades National Park to Stehekin, which is only reachable by boat or foot. We were on the boat ride back and I kept asking my dad how everything had formed–the lake and the mountains–and he was like “I don’t know, Abby–why don’t you take a geology class?” I went back to college in the fall and immediately took a geology course, which I then majored in, which led me to an internship and the National Park Service!
Other_hobbies_interests:
What hobbies don’t I have? The most serious one, which can take me away from the outdoors honestly, is theatre. I’m a playwright, director, actor and producer and have been doing theatre since I was young.
 

 I also knit, sew, bake, read, follow women’s soccer (and sometimes play) and do Scottish Country Dancing in addition to learning Scottish Gaelic. Also my undergraduate degree is in geology, so I am very interested in that.
Languages_raw: Not fluent by any means, but I have some high school Spanish. I'm also learning Scottish Gaelic, but I'm not sure that's going to help many people.

Name: Alexandra Long
Short_Bio: Alexandra is a rule-loving child turned adventurous adult who fell in love with the outdoors on hey very first backpacking trip in Wyoming’s Wind River Range. Today, she live in Pittsburgh, PA, working to protect and enhance outdoor spaces as part of the Pennsylvania Environmental Council.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors:
I was a lukewarm outdoorsy child. I was a very orderly child, who loved to follow the rules and disliked the unexpected. I loved finding patterns in the flower petals, but I despised the general chaos of nature. While in college I went on my first ever backpacking trip (after having NEVER gone camping, I might add) for six weeks in the Wind River Range of Wyoming. I can't get into why I went, but what I came out with was a deep reverence and wonder for the natural world, and, to counter my childhood hatred for anything out-of-line, an appreciation for things I know I will never make sense of. 
 

 As an adult, I have devoted my career to protecting outdoor spaces and developing inclusive, high-quality outdoor recreation assets (rail trails, river trails, MTB parks, etc). Though we have a variety of programs geared towards protecting and maintaining spaces, my favorite is a Frontline Staff Training we hold with State Park and Forest staff in the Laurel Highlands Region. We take the staff on a tour of the region, stopping at lesser-known heritage, recreation, cultural, and outdoor sites in hopes their first-hand experience in these spaces will better inform the interactions they have with visitors to their parks/forests. We hope to instill them with a place-based knowledge of the region so they can give more tailored advice to someone when asked "What's the best coffee shop close to a hiking spot?" or "Which parks have accessible parking spots and trails so I can explore with my family?" or "Where am I allowed to climb now that the Game Commission closed nearby climbing areas?" It is a highly successful program that has also deepened my own knowledge of the region.
 

 In my personal time, I enjoy recreating in those spaces I work hard to protect. Whether it is getting the run club I founded out on our local trails, taking my friends to the lesser-known climbing crag, or downhill skiing at the local hill (#skitheeast), I find joy and peace in nature. I am committed to inviting all to experience the beauty of the outdoors and helping others see past the barriers that often stop them from heading outside. I do still love to follow the rules, and will always uphold Leave No Trace, but I've come around to enjoying moments of unexpectedly hearing a wolf howling just a few miles from camp or needing to change course to avoid charismatic megafauna on the trail.
Professional_expertise_raw:
I studied at the Teton Science School in Kelly, Wyoming, creating and executing week-long trips for school-age students who visited the area. These were often focused on matching learning outcomes with spaces where topics could be covered (think: where's the best place to teach about "xyz" geological process, now go hike there!). Though I currently live in Pittsburgh PA, I can confidently still speak about the Greater Yellowstone and Grand Teton regions in regards to the natural world, what to expect while out exploring, and how to match visitors with experiences. 
 

 I currently live in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, working for the Pennsylvania Environmental Council, on developing and maintaining regional outdoor assets. I support the Industrial Heartlands Trails Coalitions, a 1000-mile proposed bike trail network as well as conservation work in the Laurel Highlands. We have programs devoted to educating staff on connecting visitors to the experiences they are hoping to have and are developing an outdoor ambassador program that will serve a similar purpose for local businesses and outfitters. I have many professional connections with guides, outfitters, recreation managers, and land owners where recreation takes place.  
 

 As for guiding, though it is never a formal part of my job description, nearly every event or conference we host includes outdoor excursions where I either lead a group, or I have coordinated the logistics of a group ride or hike.
Travel_types_experience: I have either advised or personally fall into the above categories. Through my current work and past experience I have planned trips for myself solo, taken children as young as preschool out on hikes, advised friends with babies and elderly parents on where to safely hike and camp, coordinated ultramarathon relay teams and accommodations, and taken many folks on their first adventure.

Fav_outdoor_experiences:
My monthly running club, where a few friends and I run the local single-track trails in the city park and grab pastries afterward, is one of my favorite parts of each month.
 

 Taking my dog paddleboarding for the first time on the Allegheny Reservoir and finding a secret jumping rock not far from our campsite.
 

 Finding the best local coffee shop to grab a cup and a breakfast burrito before heading out on a state forest trail run with my husband.
 

 Teaching my best friend how to ski for the first time.
Other_hobbies_interests:
I'm looking to become a certified sommelier and am writing a cookbook focused on meatball recipes that I develop in coordination with the book my book club is reading each month. This interest goes hand in hand with my appreciation for well-sourced local food, and I and a lover of gardening and CSA!
 I am incredibly passionate about how the design of a space (interior and architecture) affects human behavior. I love putting this into practice in my own home and am always working on some home improvement project!

Name: Nick Greene
Profession: Long distance backpacker and outdoor educator
Short_Bio: Nick is a thru-hiker, trail guide, and the creator of Pack & Soul, a documentary series about his 2,193-mile journey on the Appalachian Trail. He's led backcountry programs for teens, guided first-time hikers, and helped solo travelers find the confidence to get out there—whether it’s their first overnight or a multi-week trek.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors:
When I was ten, my family and I were at our winter home in western North Carolina, nestled in the mountains where the Appalachian Trail winds its way through the wilderness. One day, my brothers and I were out exploring the Trail with my grandfather when two figures emerged from the distance. They were rugged, grizzly men with wild, bushy beards, icicles clinging to their faces like badges of survival. Their massive packs seemed to swallow them whole, and their smell, well—let’s just call it unforgettable. At the time, I had no idea what I was seeing, but something about them lit a spark inside me. I didn’t know why or how, but I knew I wanted it.
 

 From that moment, the outdoors became more than just a playground—it became my inspiration, my sanctuary, my teacher, and my proving ground. It’s where I feel most alive. The natural world challenges me, humbles me, and constantly reminds me of my place in something far greater than myself. The outdoors isn’t just where I go—it’s where I become. It’s where I am most fully myself.
 

 Twenty-two years later, in 2020, I fulfilled that spark from my childhood by realizing a lifelong dream: thru-hiking all 2,193 miles of the Appalachian Trail. Over the course of four and a half months, I traversed 14 states, trekking from Georgia to Maine. The journey was nothing short of extraordinary, and one of the most rewarding aspects of the experience was creating my seven-part documentary series, PACK & SOUL, which captures every step of the adventure.
 

 On the trail, I met countless unforgettable people, but one stood out—No Rush, a trail legend who gave me the quote that would shape my life’s philosophy: “If you can’t carry it in your pack or in your soul… you don’t need it.” That wisdom remains with me to this day, a guiding principle for both the trail and life.
 

 I’m absolutely in love with the idea of Outdoorable. Before I set out on the Appalachian Trail, I sought guidance from seasoned hikers who shared their wisdom, tips, and encouragement. Their support was invaluable, helping me prepare for the challenges ahead and ultimately complete the journey. Now that I’ve walked those miles, one of my greatest joys is paying it forward—sharing my experiences, offering advice, and helping future hikers turn their dreams into reality. There’s nothing more fulfilling than seeing someone else take their first steps toward the adventure of a lifetime.
Professional_expertise_raw:
Storytelling is at the heart of everything I do. While hiking the Appalachian Trail, I channeled that passion into creating Pack & Soul, a seven-part documentary that chronicles my journey—capturing both the breathtaking beauty of the trail and the deeply personal challenges I faced along the way. It’s been amazing to see the series resonate so deeply with viewers, becoming one of the most watched on Documentary+.
 

 That experience, combined with my love for the outdoors, inspired me to start a wilderness survival program for teenagers. Through this program, I guide young people as they connect with nature, develop critical survival skills, and discover their inner strength and resilience in the wilderness.
Travel_types_experience:
As a guide, I bring a unique perspective and set of skills tailored to a variety of adventurers:
 

 For solo travelers, I understand the allure of solitude and self-discovery in the outdoors. Having spent months on the trail alone, I know how to balance the challenges of independence with the rewards of reflection and growth. I can help solo hikers find confidence in their abilities, navigate safely, and embrace the transformative power of their journey.
 

 For families with young kids, I bring both professional expertise and personal experience as a parent. As someone who is introducing my own daughter to the wonders of nature, I know how to make outdoor adventures fun, safe, and engaging for kids while creating meaningful experiences for the whole family. Whether it’s planning manageable hikes or teaching outdoor skills, I ensure that everyone, from parents to toddlers, feels included and inspired.
 

 For first-time adventurers, I have a deep appreciation for those just starting out, because I’ve been there. I remember looking up to people whose skills felt unattainable, but their guidance shaped me into the adventurer I am today. I meet new hikers where they are, breaking down intimidating challenges into achievable steps and showing them that every expert once started as a beginner. It’s incredibly rewarding to watch someone discover their potential and fall in love with the outdoors.
Geographic_areas_raw: Southern Italy / Sicily, All of Israel, Baja California
Fav_outdoor_experiences: APPALACHIAN TRAIL! I can go on and on for days about this.

Name: Rebekah Geiselman
Short_Bio: Rebekah grew up learning about the outdoors from her dad on summer trips to the Adirondacks. She reconnected with nature through solo backpacking and exploring her new home in Virginia. She's worked as a backpacking and kayak guide with REI Experiences and now teach environmental education in Los Angeles.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: My love for the environment and the outdoors began when I spent summers in the Adirondack's with my family. We had a family friend that owned a summer camp and we would stay on the property during the in between weeks and my father (a scoutmaster) would pass on all his knowledge as we got to explore. Fast forward to the pandemic when I was furloughed from my desk job and I rediscovered my love for the outdoors by exploring my new home in Virginia. I took my first backpacking trip, taught my cat how to hike, and discovered a newfound independent courage while adventuring. Fast forward another few years, and I decided to leave my desk job career behind and move out west to begin a career in outdoor education and guiding. These last 2+ years have reminded me of my summers in the adirondacks, where everything was new and exciting. And I get to share that with the kids I teach and the adults I guide.
Professional_expertise_raw: For the last 2 years I worked as a backpacking and kayak guide with REI experiences until it shut down. I’m an environmental educator with friends of the Los Angeles River where I educate children on the natural history of the LA River. I also volunteer with a local nonprofit on their hiking and camping trips with youth who have never been camping before. I have my California naturalists certification and my wilderness first responder cert.
Travel_types_experience:
The majority of my backpacking trips were solo. I’ve driven across the country twice while solo (aside from my cat). Solo adventures are honestly my favorite! 
 I’ve been working with first time adventurers for the last few years as an REI experiences guide and through in store classes. I love meeting people where they are at and assisting them gain confidence and knowledge to adventure.
Fav_outdoor_experiences: Too many to choose from! One of my favorites was a backpacking trip in point Reyes national seashore. My normal goal for a backpacking trip was to do as many miles and cover as much ground as possible. However this time, I chose to keep the mileage low and focus more on enjoying the experience. I read, journaled, meditated, and really enjoyed the solo adventure. I discovered a waterfall on the beach that I didn’t have to share with anyone else. On the last day it rained. Absolutely torrential rain, but it was my favorite day. The trail had a different feeling and I had a new sense of solitude while I was hiking out. This trip gave me a different perspective on what an “adventure” could be.

Name: Andie Cornish
Short_Bio: Andie grew up in Jackson, Wyoming, with the Tetons as her backyard and playground. She now travels the world as a professional trail runner and elite ski mountaineer.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: Born and raised in Jackson, Wyoming, I have spent most of my life in the outdoors. With Grand Teton National Park as my backyard, I am lucky enough to spend my summers running in the Tetons and my winters skiing those same mountains. I am now a professional trail runner, running and competing at the highest international level, and an elite skier/ski mountaineer. My time spent in the mountains is incredibly important to me, and has shaped much of who I am. As an environmental studies major at the Unviersity of Vermont, I am deeply passionate about protecting these outdoor spaces. I want to use my platform and expertise as a professional athlete to help other people explore and experience these wonderful outdoor spaces.
Professional_expertise_raw: As mentioned on the last page, I am a professional trail runner and avid ski mountaineer/mountain biker/gravel biker. My experience as a professional athlete has allowed me to travel and explore many different international locations, allowing me to provide great expertise on travel and outdoor adventure. I also work with the Teton Backcountry alliance in Jackson, as a Teton Pass Ambassador. Promote responsible recreation and ensure safety on and around Teton Pass. Provide assistance to skiers and riders as needed while maintaining open communication with WYDOT to ensure efficient parking lot operations. Support rescue efforts during accidents and avalanche incidents. Serve as the primary point of contact for all individuals engaging in skiing and riding activities on Teton Pass. Here I promote responsible recreation and ensure safety on and around Teton Pass. Assist skiers and riders as needed while maintaining open communication with WYDOT to ensure efficient parking lot operations. Support rescue efforts during accidents and avalanche incidents. Serve as the primary point of contact for all individuals engaging in skiing and riding activities on Teton Pass.
Travel_types_experience: I spend most of my year travelling to different national and international locations for training and racing, allowing me to truly experience the culture and adventure these locations offer. From Norway to Belize, I have spent time staying, eating, running, and recreating in diverse locations, all while keeping track of the places I loved like restaurants, trails, sights to see etc. I have traveled to a lot of these places alone, as well as with my family (multi-gen), first-time adventure friends, and of course, many extreme athletes.
Geographic_areas_raw:
USA: Wyoming, entire Jackson Hole area and surrounding vicinity. Also extremely familiar with the entire state of Wyoming. (Grand Teton, Wind Rivers, Jackson Hole Mountain Resort, Teton Pass etc). 
 USA: Idaho: Driggs/Victor, Sun Valley:Galena Pass, Sawtooth Mountains 
 USA: Utah: Salt Lake City, Park City
 USA: Vermont: Extremely familiar with entire state, went to school at UVM. Green USA: Mountains, all fall activities (very into fall), Mad River Valley, the Long Trail
 Norway: Southers:Oslo, South West: Hemsedal (summer running trip) , Andalsnes (fjord skiing trip) Alesund
 Switzerland: Veysonnaz, Crans Montana
 France: Chamonix, Paris
 Denmark: Copenhagen
Fav_outdoor_experiences: Oh man, it is so hard to choose! I recently came back from Andalsnes in southwestern Norway, where I did a ski moutaineering trip in the Fjords. It was my first time getting to ski in this area, and I loved it. Norway is a truly spectacular place, and the entire Rauma municipality has so much to offer. Every day, we got to ski in a different location, meeting new people and seeing different mountain ranges. The cabin we stayed in was a beautiful, cozy, small Norwegian ski in ski out cabin. We spent so much time exploring the outdoors and the small local community of shops and foods as well. Some of my favorite memories obviously also come from my time at home in Grand Teton National Park. Two years ago I ran (no ropes) up and down the Grand Teton with some friends, which was an amazing experience. I have run and skied all over those peaks and know every canyon, trail, and coulior that exists. Running the Grand for the first time was a truly magical experience, allowing me to stand a top a mountain that means so much to me. I could go on forever, but I am definitely extremely lucky to have experienced what I have experienced so far in my life.
Other_hobbies_interests: I am really into crafting; I love DIY projects and creating things I can use as decor in my house. I love spending time with my dogs, drinking coffee, watching documentaries, being with friends, and trying new foods/experiencing new cultures.
Languages_raw: I am semi-proficient in Spanish. I took it my whole life, and can converse in some general conversation if needed.

Name: Maria Lally
Short_Bio: Maria has been a Park Ranger and outdoor educator at some of the country’s most iconic national parks, including Glacier, Grand Canyon, and Rocky Mountain. If you’re planning a national park trip and need help navigating seasonal conditions, permits, trail choices, or even picking which park to visit, she's got you covered.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I was first drawn to the outdoors when traveling to national parks with my family when I was young. One of the most transformative moments was hiking to Vernal Falls in Yosemite and being awestruck by the waterfall's immense beauty. It was the first hike I'd ever done in my life, and at that moment I had no idea there would be thousands more! I have worked as a Park Ranger and Education Technician for the National Park Service throughout my career, at parks such as Glacier, Grand Canyon, and Rocky Mountain, where I connect visitors with the outdoors every day. I spend my free time hiking, backpacking, whitewater rafting, and traveling. I have also sought outdoor adventure abroad, and have trekked through Patagonia, lived out of a campervan in New Zealand, backpacked through Southeast Asia, climbed the tallest mountain in Ireland, and more!
Professional_expertise_raw: As previously mentioned, I have worked as a Park Ranger and Education Technician for the National Park Service at Glacier, Grand Canyon, and Rocky Mountain. I have also worked as an outdoor guide in Rocky Mountain. I have planned and designed both short-term and long term travels for both myself and others. I have a Wilderness First Aid certification and am hoping to get my Wilderness First Responder soon!
Travel_types_experience: Through my work as a Park Ranger, I interact with diverse audiences within all of these travel types daily, providing recommendations and planning their trips. I have traveled solo and am very familiar with solo travel safety, accommodations, transportation, and more worldwide. I have had various partners and friends who have been extreme athletes, so I am very familiar with extreme sport opportunities, especially ultra running.

Name: Andreas Frese
Profession: Outdoor Educator and van life expert
Short_Bio: Andreas has travelled all across the US, while living the “van life”, mapped out long-distance trails, hiked thousands of miles across five continents, and led groups ranging from first-timers to seasoned hikers.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: From playing in the woods and riding my bike as a child, to hiking, kayaking and mountain biking as a young adult, to living out of my truck travelling around the country, backpacking, thru hiking, and world travel in my middle age the outdoors has been my place of enjoyment, peace, fitness, therapy, and connection with nature and my inner self.
Professional_expertise_raw:
While my profession has been in other fields outside of the office I have gained experience and knowledge in the outdoors through
 - Planning, organizing, and leading group trips through platforms like Meetup
 - Planning my own adventures including months long trip across the globe
 - Volunteering with the USFS as a trail adopter
 - Training in Wilderness First Aid
 - Thru hiking long distance trails
 - Planning, researching on the ground, documenting, and creating a web site for a new long distance trail
 - Aiding friends and acquaintances in planning their own adventure travels
 - Aiding friends in reaching their own hiking challenges

Travel_types_experience:

I've also travelled to Iceland three times with a friend. 
 I've lead group hikes through Meetup and also through work outings with people ranging from 18 to 80.

I've hiked with 20 year olds that struggle and 70 year olds that run like a jack rabbit up a steep slope. We all slow down a bit as we get older but it is never too late to get in better shape.
 Through leading hikes with Meetup, and helping friends get into hiking or adventure travel, I've shepherded many budding adventurers to achieve their goals.

Fav_outdoor_experiences:
Wow, there are so many, but here are just a few.
 Living out of my truck for four months, driving across the country, and visiting many National Parks for the first time.
 Hiking Half Dome in Yosemite NP out of season with "cables down".
 Climbing to just shy of 20,000 feet (6000+ meters) on Huayna Potosi in Bolivia.
 Hiking 700 miles from Mexico to the bottom of the Grand Canyon.
 Hanging over 1000 feet above the valley floor on the side of a cliff in Murren, Switzerland.
 Hiking 50 miles in Iceland on the Laugavegur and Fimmvorduhals trails.
 Coming across large herds of wild animals with no one else around whether its reindeer in Norway, or Roosevelt Elk below Mt. Saint Helens.
 Seeing an animal in the wild for the very first time.

Name: Mary Leavines
Short_Bio: Mary achieved a lifelong goal of thru-hiking the entire Appalachian Trail as a solo female hiker. Since then, she's completed 650+ miles of the Pacific Crest Trail, owned and operated a thru-hiker hostel, and guided backpacking trips across the Blue Ridge Mountains and beyond.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: I’ve always loved the outdoors, and in 2022, I achieved a long-held dream by thru-hiking the Appalachian Trail, as a solo woman. Since then, I’ve completed 650+ miles of the Pacific Crest Trail, owned and operated a thru-hiker hostel, and am currently in grad school to become a therapist. My long distance hikes, and anytime I’m able to get outdoors, have had huge benefits for my mental health, confidence, and even my career trajectory as a student and professional backpacking guide.
Professional_expertise_raw: Thru-hike: Appalachian Trail, long distance hike, Pacific Crest Trail. Wilderness First Aid and Mental Health First Aid certified. Professional history counseling aspiring thru-hikers with online freelance coaching and in-person rapport when I owned a hiker hostel in Franklin, NC. Current guide for Blue Ridge Hiking Company in Asheville.
Travel_types_experience: While I have helped older generations, I find that I’m most helpful to middle-aged women who are venturing out on their own, often for the first time.
Geographic_areas_raw:
Appalachian trail: lived and worked near the Georgia - Virginia section: hiked all sections of the AT, Georgia to Maine
 

 East coast: North Carolina : Tennessee: Smoky Mtns National Park
 

 West coast: Utah : southern california : Pacific Northwest
 

 Maine: Baxter state park: Acadia national park: Portland
Fav_outdoor_experiences: Anytime can knock off a long distance trail, I’m happy! But I particularly love road trips, exploring the blue ridge parkway, and finding off the beaten path spots as a local adventurer.
Other_hobbies_interests: Photography, writing, reading, motorcycle riding

Name: Owen Eigenbrot
Short_Bio: Owen has hiked over 13,000 miles since 2015, including some of the most rugged and rewarding long-distance trails in the world. Whether you’re planning your first backpacking trip or a thru-hike, he can help with route selection, gear, logistics, and trail confidence so you can focus on the adventure, not the stress.
What_drew_you_to_outdoors: It was my parents who first brought me outside on camping trips and hikes before I was even able to walk. With that start, it was a no-brainer to keep it up by joining the Boy Scouts and sticking with the program through high school. It was what I loved to do, and all my best friends were involved as well. As an adult, backpacking, climbing, and traveling weren't as much of a given, but after a few years, I circled back and built my life around recreating outdoors. I moved near the mountains, then eventually gave up my engineering career because being desk-bound bummed me out. Now, backpacking, thru-hiking, trail running, and skiing are all I want to do. Although, I still spend more time than I want typing at my computer, I'm fortunate to have had the opportunity to make long-distance hiking a core aspect of my life. Since 2015, I've walked over 13,000 miles and been able to share my experiences through my writing. I've also spent several years working in customer service in the outdoor industry, which I love because it keeps me directly connected with the wider community. Whether it's gear advice, outdoor skills, or just talking about our favorite trips, sharing dialogue with others is endlessly rewarding for me.
Professional_expertise_raw:
I'm a reformed aerospace engineer who now writes professionally about backpacking and works as the customer service rep at a small ultralight gear manufacturer, Katabatic Gear. Most of my writing has been for the online backpacking journal, TheTrek.co, where I even filled in as interim Managing Editor for six months while the incumbent was on sabbatical. This included not just producing my own content about thru-hiking (news stories, gear guides, trail guides, cultural pieces), but also managing the editorial team, gear reviews, and freelance writers. I've also written extensively for my personal blog, hikefordays.com, over the past decade, through which I have also begun a one-on-one coaching program to help everyone and anyone feel more comfortable outside. Additionally, I've had my outdoor photography featured in several calendars and worked the sales floor at REI.
 And this summer I'm hoping to finally make the jump into guiding multi-day backpacking trips. Working through the interview process now.
Travel_types_experience:
Solo travelers: I've been a solo traveler for most of my life. I have made 30+ solo backpacking trips over the years, and took myself to Nepal for a month when I was 21 years old. Ever since that trip, I've never let being alone slow me down and I have traveled internationally extensively solo and backpacked hundreds of nights alone.
 

 Travelers aged 60+: I'm only 34, but I've done a lot of backpacking and traveling with my parents, who are both now 70. My biggest trips have been hiking to Annapurna Base Camp in Nepal with my mom and hiking the John Muir Trail with her last summer. Both were eye-opening for me as an able-bodied youth and now I will never take smooth trail for granted.
 

 First-time adventurers: Through my work in customer service and backcountry coaching, I interact with a lot of novice hikers/backpackers. I always aim to be helpful, humble, and open to listening. I've also gone on a number of trips with friends who hadn't been backpacking before.
 

 Extreme athletes: I'm not sure if long thru-hikes qualify as 'extreme' but if so, then I have a lot of experience with extreme athletes and love to talk about thru-hiking with anyone who will listen. With several longer trips in my past, I have a lot of experience to offer in the realm of long-distance backpacking, whether it is considered extreme or not.
Geographic_areas_raw: Sierra Nevada, Colorado Rockies, Grand Canyon, Mount Hood, Mount Rainier, Yellowstone, New Mexico, Florida, The Appalachians, Annapurna Region, Solukhumbu District, Kathmandu, Pokhara, Wales, The Alps, Baja Peninsula, Puerto Vallarta, Manzanillo, Yucatán Peninsula, New Brunswick, Quebec.
Fav_outdoor_experiences: I've had so many great experiences while backpacking and traveling, and perhaps the most rewarding was hiking the Sierra High Route (SHR). That one had been on my list for a long time, and finally getting to give it a try in 2021 was the culmination of a lot of experience building, planning, and luck. And to have my partner with me was a major bonus. It was one of my hardest trips, and also the most rewarding. Every day, even during the hard ones, I was filled with gratitude. The mosquitos sucked and we almost got struck by lightning, but it was one of the richest and freest experiences of my life.
Languages_raw: A little bit of Spanish, but I would be embarrassed to try.
